category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The helicopter displays a warning light for engine failure while the flight crew is en route back to base after completing a patient transport. The pilot warns the medical crew that there will be a hard landing and delegates to the medic to contact ATC to declare an emergency while the pilot attempts to maintain control of the aircraft for an autorotation. The crew's likelihood of surviving is most affected by the
options: Number of hours the pilot has as PIC
Distance to the closest fire station
Amount of survival supplies available in the aircraft
rationale: The number one influence on a crew's survival is having a positive attitude and a hopeful outlook on the situation. Survivors will also need survival gear, shelter, food and water, etc. but holding the belief that you will be rescued is imperative to survival. If you apply this concept to your desire to pass your advanced certification exam, you have to believe in yourself. Yes, you still need to study, you still need to work on practice questions and learn from your mentors, but believing that you can and will pass the test will help you so much! Don't get me wrong, this is a difficult exam, but YOU ARE CAPABLE!

stem: The flight crew is requested to respond to the scene of a single vehicle crash in which the driver was ejected from the vehicle and found 25 feet from the car. Upon entering the ambulance, the flight crew finds the ground paramedic attempting IV access and the EMT is assisting ventilations through an extraglottic airway. The flight nurse notices the patient is in sinus tachycardia at a rate of 152, the SpO₂ is 71%, and the blood pressure is currently cycling. The patient then goes into pulseless V-tach. In addition to high quality CPR, the priority intervention is to:
options: Perform a needle thoracostomy and place a pelvic binder on the patient
Replace the extraglottic airway with an endotracheal tube
rationale: Two causes of traumatic arrest that can be quickly treated are a tension pneumothorax and a pelvic fracture. Decompressing the chest bilaterally via needle or finger thoracostomy will allow for full lung expansion and reduction in intrathoracic pressure, which will facilitate venous return to the heart and improve cardiac output. Placing a pelvic binder can tamponade the vascular compromise and reduce bleeding in the pelvis caused by an open book pelvic fracture, which can account for >1 liter of blood loss. All of the other options are necessary interventions, but not the *top* priority.

stem: A 32-year-old female is being transported to the high risk OB center for severe hypertension in pregnancy. She is 35w6d with appropriate prenatal care and no known fetal complications, but today her OB sent her to the local ED due to a severe headache and blood pressure of 166/92. During the flight, the patient begins to seize. The most appropriate medication is:
rationale: The first line medication to control seizures in the hypertensive pregnant patient is a 4-6 gram magnesium bolus over 15-20 minutes followed by an infusion of 2-4 grams per hour. It is imperative to maintain oxygenation >95% and to reduce blood pressure to 140/90mmHg, which may require a bolus of antihypertensive after the magnesium is initiated.

stem: The flight paramedic is reviewing the patient's transfer paperwork that was given to the flight crew by the sending facility. The patient is being transported from an ICU in Nashville, TN to an ICU in Seattle, WA via fixed wing for urosepsis. She is currently intubated and stable on the mechanical ventilator and her vital signs are: temperature 37.9° C, HR 92, NIBP 111/65, ETCO₂ 34, SpO₂ 94%. The ABG drawn 6 hours ago was pH 7.19, CO₂ 28, HCO₃ 16, PaO₂ 72. As the crew was departing the ICU, the RT caught them and told the crew she had just run another ABG and the pH is 7.30. The most appropriate action is to:
options: Perform a point of care serum chemistry test
rationale: There is an inversely proportional relationship between potassium and pH. This patient had a serum potassium of 3.9 hours ago when the patient was severely acidotic, but the pH has increased significantly, which necessitates rechecking the potassium with a plan to replace the expected drop with a potassium infusion. Most fixed wing programs carry point of care testing equipment that allows for a basic chemistry to be run. On rotor, it's not as urgent because they travel relatively short distances, but on fixed wing transports going from one side of the country to the other, electrolytes cannot wait to be corrected.

stem: The patient exhibits Kehr's sign. A possible injury is:
options: An open femur fracture following a fall from a roof
A subdural hematoma in a patient on eliquis
A splenic laceration following an MVC
rationale: Kehr's sign is referred pain to the left shoulder most often caused by an injury to the spleen. If a patient is complaining of left shoulder pain and mechanism for abdominal injury, the clinician should have a high index of suspicion for a splenic injury and prioritize rapid transport to a facility with OR capabilities and to manage hemodynamic stability during transport using blood products.

stem: The flight crew arrives at the bedside of a patient with a head injury after a ground level fall. The patient has a history of heavy alcohol use, untreated hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and cirrhosis of the liver. On assessment, the crew observes a unilateral fixed and dilated pupil and the vitals are: HR 54, NIBP 149/51, SpO₂ 92%, ETCO₂ 34. The flight nurse:
rationale: The patient is exhibiting signs of impending brain herniation (unilateral blown pupil and Cushing's Triad). Administering a bolus of hypertonic saline will temporarily reduce cerebral edema and buy the patient time to get to definitive care. The way this works is by using peer pressure in your favor. The brain cells like to match the tonicity of the vasculature around it and if we introduce a hypertonic solution (3% saline) it will shift water out of the cell to concentrate the intracellular solutes, thus making the cell smaller. This only lasts a couple of hours, and the kidneys will fix the serum osmolality and the brain cells will go right back to swelling, so prioritize rapid transport to definitive care.

stem: A normal potassium level is:
rationale: Normal potassium: 3.5-5.0 meq/L. Normal sodium: 135-145 meq/L, Normaal Ph: 7.35-7.45. Normal Calcium:8.5-10.2 mg/dL.

stem: The transport nurse is caring for a patient with acute pancreatitis. In addition to antiemetics, fluid bolus, and analgesics, the nurse expects to administer:
rationale: Patients with pancreatitis often have accompanying electrolyte imbalances, notably hypocalcemia. The transport provider should watch for Trousseau's sign, carpopedal spasms during inflation of the blood pressure cuff isolated to the arm that the cuff is on.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A flight program has established multiple safeguards to reduce the likelihood of safety compromise during flight operations. The flight nurse knows the organization is using:
rationale: The Swiss Cheese Model creates multiple layers of protection that by themselves are imperfect, but when they are combined, the theory is that the holes in the cheese will not align, thus creating full safety coverage.

stem: The flight crew arrives at the bedside in the ICU and finds the patient has a Swan-Ganz line in place with the following readings: CVP 11, PA 28/17, CI 1.9, SVR 1289. What is the most appropriate action?
rationale: This patient is in cardiogenic shock, as evidenced by the low cardiac index and high PA, and CVP - the blood is backing up into the right side of the heart because the left side is unable to push it forward. The best treatment for this patient is a milrinone infusion, a positive inotrope to improve the contractility of the heart. The milrinone will also help to reduce the SVR a bit so the left heart doesn't have as much afterload to push against. The norepinephrine infusion is not appropriate because it will serve to increase SVR, further worsening the workload on the left heart. The NS bolus will worsen the congested right atrium, and increasing the epinephrine infusion will increase SVR.

stem: The flight crew lands at the LZ to rendezvous with a BLS crew to transport a 78-year-old female whose husband called 911 today because she was mumbling about penguins living in their basement and she urinated in the kitchen trash can thinking it was the restroom. The BLS crew checked a glucose (196 g/dL) and they performed a 12 lead EKG, which appears normal. Her vital signs are: Temperature 37.6° C, HR 70, NIBP 128/63, ETCO₂ 31, RR 21, SpO₂ 97%. Her only significant history is hypertension, which she takes daily metoprolol for. The crew recognizes that this is likely:
rationale: Urinary tract infections classically present with confusion in the elderly. You may have been thrown off by the normal vital signs, but always consider what's "normal" vs. normal for THIS patient. This patient has a history of hypertension, so this is likely hypotensive for her. Why isn't she tachycardic? She is on a beta-blocker for her blood pressure, but that will also limit her ability to increase her heart rate, so she is unable to compensate with tachycardia. Suspect and further investigate for shock in non-tachycardic patients who are on a beta blocker when the rest of the clinical picture points to shock, even if it is weak evidence. This wasn't a question about urosepsis, this was a question to see if you can think outside the box to recognize sepsis when it is disguised by various circumstances.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The transport team is responding to a 911 call from a store owner for a man lounging on the sidewalk in front of her storefront. Police are on scene and speaking to the man whose speech is slurred, he has an empty bottle of hard liquor in his hand, and when he attempts to stand, he stumbles. He tells the crew "I'm fine, I don't need to go to the hospital! They are mean to me there!" The crew's priority is to:
options: Respect his right to refuse care
rationale: Patients that chronically abuse alcohol are at a high risk for a subdural hemorrhage, so it is imperative to perform a neuro assessment on all intoxicated patients. While the patient does have the right to refuse care, the crew should assess for capacity to refuse, and from the information given in this question, there is indication that he is not stable enough to refuse care. The patient's blood glucose should be checked before giving dextrose, and restraining him would be battery because, with the information given in the question, the crew is not justified in restraining the patient, as he does not pose a threat to himself, the crew, or others.

stem: The flight crew needs to perform a needle thoracostomy to decompress a tension pneumothorax. Which of the following best describes the lateral approach?
options: 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, inserting the needle above the rib
2nd-3rd intercostal space, midclavicular line, inserting below the rib
4th-5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line, inserting on top of the rib and riding up over it
4th-5th intercostal space, midaxillary line, inserting below the rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle
rationale: There is a much higher failure rate when attempting a needle thoracostomy with an anterior approach (2-3 ICS midclavicular line) than with a lateral approach. To decompress a pneumothorax using the lateral approach, insert the needle at the 4th-5th intercostal space anterior axillary line (front of the armpit zone) on the rib, then after striking the bone, ride up and over the top of rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle that runs along the bottom side of the rib.

stem: The patient's nurse at the sending facility tells the transport team that the patient's lab work revealed hyaline casts. The transport anticipates the need for:
rationale: Hyaline casts are found in the urine of severely dehydrated patients and in those with chronic and progressive renal failure. It's essentially a tubular booger from fluid moving sluggishly through the kidney and the presence of increased glomerular permeability.

stem: The flight nurse notices that the patient's fingers become rigid and straight with flexed wrists when the blood pressure cuff inflates, and prepares to give:
rationale: Trousseau's sign is carpopedal spasms during blood pressure cuff inflation (only affecting the arm with the cuff) and is indicative of hypocalcemia. To treat hypocalcemia, the clinician can administer calcium gluconate through a peripheral IV, or can give calcium chloride through a central line (or large bore peripheral IV) because calcium chloride is a vesicant and can cause tissue necrosis. Calcium chloride has 3.5 times the elemental calcium as calcium gluconate, therefore a larger dose is required when giving calcium gluconate.

stem: A fixed wing crew is requested to transport a patient diagnosed with severe malnutrition secondary to anorexia nervosa to a specialty center 4 hours away. The crew prioritizes:
options: Bringing finger foods for the patient to eat in flight
Reviewing the patient's lab work and performing an EKG
Intubating this patient prior to leaving the sending facility
rationale: Severely malnourished patients are notoriously electrolyte depleted and a common and dangerous electrolyte disturbance in patients with anorexia nervosa is hypokalemia, which would require potassium replacement; severe hypokalemia can cause torsades de pointes because its direct electrophysiological effects include resting membrane hyperpolarization and reduced repolarization reserve.

stem: The flight crew is preparing to intubate a 14kg child with chest trauma after being ejected from an ATV he was a passenger on. To facilitate intubation, the flight medic suggests using ketamine (500mg/10mL) and rocuronium (100mg/10mL). When preparing the medications, the transport nurse:
options: Mixes the sedation and paralytic into one single syringe so the meds can be pushed together
Knows to double the dose of induction agent in pediatric patients
Tells the medic that ketamine is unsafe in pediatric patients under 20kg and suggests using etomidate instead
Dilutes the medications to reduce the concentration
rationale: Giving small doses of concentrated medication leaves little margin for error when drawing up tiny volumes. Diluting medications by pulling up 1mL of medication and adding it to 9mL in a flush changes the concentration by a factor of 10. The ketamine that was originally 500mg in 10mL (50mg/mL) is now 50mg in 10mL (5mg/mL) which is much safer when attempting to draw up pediatric doses. Mixing these concentrations is easily facilitated by using a stop cock to move 1mL of original concentration into the 10mL flush that had 1mL squirted out. Use the vials of pretend medications in the education closet and practice doing this. Create scenarios for yourself using your RSI protocols and practice this until you're comfortable with it.

stem: When reviewing the patient's lab work from the sending facility, the paramedic sees the patient has an elevated BUN with a normal creatinine. The paramedic prioritizes:
options: Rapid transport to a hospital with dialysis capabilities
Transport to a PCI capable facility
rationale: Dehydration will cause elevated levels of BUN (blood urea nitrogen, normal is 6-20), but with healthy functioning kidneys the creatinine will be normal (0.8-1.2). The priority treatment for the dehydrated patient is a crystalloid bolus. Dialysis is not indicated because the normal creatinine indicates proper renal function; there is no indication of acute myocardial infarction in the information given, and a diuretic would worsen this patient's condition.

stem: The flight crew rendezvous with an ALS crew for a 23-year-old male patient complaining of throat pain, weakness, and lethargy for the last 3 days. The patient's vital signs are: temperature 38.9° C, HR 131 (sinus tachycardia), NIBP 107/71, ETCO₂ 29, SpO₂ 95%, RR 34 and shallow. The patient is spitting into an emesis bag and tells the crew in a hoarse voice that he cannot swallow. Upon assessment, the patient's throat appears very swollen and the tonsils are in contact with each other. The flight crew discusses intubating the patient for airway patency during the flight, and decides on all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Giving a 500mL fluid bolus while setting up to intubate
Bypass intubation attempt and move to surgical cricothyrotomy
Prepare push-dose pressors for use in the peri-intubation phase
rationale: This patient is tachycardic and borderline hypotensive, which should alert the crew to a shock index of >1.0 (associated with higher rates of peri-intubation cardiac arrest) and give a fluid bolus with push dose pressors readily available. While this is very likely shock, the crew should also recognize that fever will induce tachycardia and plan to give an antipyretic during transport (however this is not an immediate peri-intubation priority). As evidenced by the significant tonsil swelling, hoarse voice, and inability to manage secretions, this patient requires urgent airway management. Adult males should be intubated with a 8.0 endotracheal tube unless there are expected complications, and in this case it will be difficult to pass an 8.0 tube through the edematous airway, but the crew should attempt intubation before moving to a surgical cricothyrotomy (if there was no ventilation/oxygenation, that would be an indication to move directly to surgical airway management).

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The pilot in command is briefing the medical crew at the start of the night shift at a rural base that does frequent flights into a large urban area. He states "As we approach the city, I want one of you off night vision goggles." The medical crew knows this is because
options: Not all light is visible through NVGs
Fog will obstruct the view through the night vision goggles
The pilot wants to avoid the crew members developing tunnel vision
The pilot wants one crew member watching the patient while the other crew member looks out for hazards
rationale: Night vision goggles amplify light 40,000x and allows users to visualize hazards and landing zone obstacles, but a deficiency is that some light-emitting diodes (LED lights) are not visible through NVGs. Some towers have LED lights and can only be seen with the naked eye, so it is important to intermittently look around the NVGs while wearing them, or dedicate one crew member to be off goggles to look for warning lights on towers that are LED.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The transport team has a critical patient on board and is struck from behind at a busy intersection about one mile from the accepting hospital. The crew recognizes the priority is to:
options: Move the ambulance to a safe area out of the intersection
Call a supervisor to notify them of the collision
Call for another ambulance to complete the patient transport
Focus on patient care until the police arrive
rationale: While it is important to call for an ambulance so the patient transport can be completed and to notify a supervisor, it is an immediate priority to move the ambulance out of the intersection to avoid further danger to the patient, crew, and ambulance. The crew can continue patient care while they await assistance from PD, another ambulance, a supervisor, etc.

stem: During transport, the patient's Swan-Ganz line is in place with the following readings: CVP 12, PA 27/16, CO 3.9, CI 1.7, SVR 1310 and the vital signs are: temperature 37.2° C, HR 106, NIBP 139/89, SpO₂ 95%, ETCO₂ 28. The transport nurse recognizes:
options: The need to reduce the respiratory rate
The patient is in cardiogenic shock
The patient is stable and requires no changes
The patient is in early compensated septic shock
rationale: This patient is in cardiogenic shock, which may not be obvious because of the normotension (blood pressure isn't the only indicator of shock!). However, the cardiac index is low (normal is 2.5-4.0) and the capnography is low (normal is 35-45) which indicates poor tissue perfusion. The novice provider may want to drop the respiratory rate to hold more CO2 to fix the hypocapnia, but this is not a hyperventilation problem. CO2 is a waste product of cellular metabolism, which the body cannot adequately perform in this state due to the poor perfusion. This patient is not in early compensated septic shock; in that case we would expect to see a normal cardiac output and cardiac index.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The transport team arrives at the scene of a house fire in which the mother ran back into the house multiple times to get her children, pets, and a few family heirlooms out. She is refusing care and states "I'm fine, I need to stay here, I can't go to the hospital!" The paramedic should:
options: Prioritize assessing the children, as she is obviously not affected because she is able to speak clearly and in full sentences
Allow her to sign a refusal as long as her vital signs are stable and she has no carbonaceous sputum
Fully assess the patient and encourage her to be transported to the ED
Chemically sedate her in order to safely load her in the ambulance for transport to the nearest burn center
rationale: This patient seems fine right now because her body has endogenous epinephrine surging through her circulation. She just performed acts of maternal heroism, and she is in full sympathetic overdrive, which is serving her well to compensate for potential airway compromise. However, when the lights and sirens clear the scene and the vibe simmers, she will lose that catecholamine dump and she will quickly lose her airway patency. Inhalation burn patients do great for a short time, but they have a penchant for rapid decline. If the crew has any suspicion of impending airway compromise, it is best to intubate early.

stem: The flight crew is preparing to intubate a 17-year-old boy with Treacher Collins syndrome due to respiratory distress secondary to aspiration pneumonia following a recent choking incident. The crew knows that:
options: They should use video laryngoscopy without first attempting direct laryngoscopy and that it is likely they will be unable to place an endotracheal tube
Direct laryngoscopy is recommended over video laryngoscopy for endotracheal intubation in this patient population
This patient's anatomy favors being placed in Trendelenburg position for endotracheal intubation
The mechanical ventilator settings should include very low PEEP and an extended expiratory time for this patient
rationale: Treacher Collins syndrome is a genetic condition that presents with underdeveloped facial features, cleft palate, and anatomical hypopharyngeal obstruction which leads to positional dyspnea. These patients have a high degree of difficulty when intubating and video laryngoscopy greatly improves the view for ETT placement. In many cases, a supraglottic airway is placed due to the difficulty of placing an ETT.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A patient's blood gas reads: pH 6.27, CO₂ 28, HCO₃ 14, PaO₂ 68. The transport nurse recognizes the patient is in:
options: Fully compensated metabolic acidosis with hypoxia
rationale: The low pH indicates acidosis, and the low bicarb (HCO3 normal 22-26) places the blame for the acidosis on the metabolic process. The CO2 (normal 35-45) noticed the low bicarb and attempted to balance the pH by blowing off CO2, but was not able to fully restore the pH into a normal range, so it is partially compensated. A normal PaO2 is 80-100, so this patient does have moderate hypoxia.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight paramedic has a moderate headache while at 1200 feet AGL on a 50 minute rotor flight back to base after transporting a patient at <2000 feet AGL. An extrinsic factor that is likely contributing to his headache is:
options: He did not have time to eat lunch before being toned out on the flight
He did not sleep well last night and has been groggy in flight
rationale: Rotorcraft vibrations significantly contribute to headaches, neck/back pain, and increased fatigue. The crew did not fly at a high enough altitude to experience a significant enough pressure change to induce hypoxia; low blood glucose related to missed lunch and grogginess are intrinsic factors, but both could lend to the paramedic's headache.

stem: The flight nurse recognizes the patient most likely to be affected by altitude changes in flight is:
options: A 4-year-old who had two febrile seizures in the last 90 minutes
The 22-year-old patient with a femur fracture after an ATV rollover
The 33-year-old patient with an open skull fracture after an assault
rationale: Boyle's law describes the inversely proportional (one goes up, the other goes down) relationship between the volume of a gas and the pressure exerted on the gas. There is less pressure at altitude, therefore gasses will expand. The patient most likely to have trapped gas is the patient with the open skull fracture. A pneumocephalus (translates: air brain) will expand at altitude and increase intracranial pressure, worsening the patient's neurologic status. This patient should be flown in a pressurized cabin or at minimum safe altitude by rotor to prevent complications.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The pilot asks the flight medic to make radio contact with the fire crew that established the landing zone at the scene of a multi-car accident as they are five minutes out. It is dark with low moonlight; the flight medic knows the priority is to:
options: Tell the LZ crew what direction the aircraft will be approaching from after performing a high and low recon
Obtain details about the landing zone, such as hazards and other inbound aircraft
Obtain patient information, including current condition and the estimated patient weight
Ask for the LZ crew to turn on strobing lights to guide the aircraft in
rationale: Communication with the LZ crew is primarily to learn about hazards that pose danger to the aircraft so the pilot can mitigate risks. Knowing where power lines are, if there are other aircraft approaching the scene, being aware of slopes, and pointing out light poles allows the pilot to plan the safest approach and avoid wire strikes or collision with obstacles. Obtaining patient information is helpful, but it is not the priority and asking for strobe lights would worsen night vision and affect NVG use.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A flight nurse observes her paramedic partner giving the patient a medication that the patient refused. The nurse recognizes that this is:
options: Acceptable, given the patient's critical status
rationale: Assault is when a medical provider threatens harm to a patient, and battery is when unconsented contact is made, such as administering a medication or touching a patient after they have refused the intervention. Malpractice is when a healthcare provider renders substandard care. The nursing exams ask about this quite a bit, but it's important to know for each discipline regarding safe and respectful patient care.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight paramedic notices the patient's respiratory rate changes from 17 to 21 after the patient is loaded into the aircraft and the aircraft lifts to 1500 feet AGL. The paramedic:
options: Recognizes this is likely early respiratory distress and starts a duo-neb treatment
Suspects hypoxia related to the altitude change and places the patient on supplementary oxygen
Understands increased respiratory rate is a normal change associated with rotor transport
rationale: Being onboard a running rotorcraft and climbing to altitude induces several expected changes, including an increase in respiratory rate, mild dehydration, an increased metabolic rate, and dry mucous membranes. Crews should be aware of these impacts on themselves and with patients.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The medical crew knows the only reason to speak during critical phases of flight is:
options: To communicate with each other about patient care
To communicate hazards or safety concerns to the pilot
To inform the pilot that the crew needs to restock medical supplies prior to accepting another flight
rationale: Sterile cockpit dictates that there should be no talking during critical phases of flight except information pertaining to the safety of the flight. Describing obstacles and their location, bringing hazards to the pilot's attention, or calling for a go-around are acceptable reasons to talk during take off, landing, taxiing, and refueling.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight nurse is giving radio report for a patient being transported from a rural emergency department to a level 1 trauma center that receives many transfers from outlying facilities per day. He states the patient's name so that the receiving facility knows which patient the nurse is talking about. This is:
options: A violation of the patient's privacy, as radio channels are not a secure form of communication
Acceptable because the receiving facility has the patient's name and demographics that were sent during the acceptance process from the sending facility
Improves the communication with the nurse receiving radio report because it allows her to differentiate from other inbound transfers
rationale: Radios are not a secure line of communication and are often monitored by apps and the general public. Protected health information that could be used to identify the patient should never be communicated on radio channels. An acceptable radio report identifying the transfer using military alphabet might sound like "We are en route with patient Kilo Sierra, are you aware of this patient?" Of note, if calling report by satellite phone, it is acceptable to give the patient's identifiers, but not essential.

stem: The transport crew recognizes a timing error on the patient's intra-aortic balloon pump. They should:
options: Assume it is an error if the patient's vital signs are stable
Power off the machine and manually inflate the balloon once every 3--5 minutes
Inspect the tubing for rust colored flakes
Switch the trigger from EKG to pressure
rationale: Most modern balloon pumps are capable of making necessary changes without input from the providers, but one change that can be made to correct a timing error is to change the trigger. This is especially helpful when the patient is in arrhythmias, such as atrial fibrillation.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The crew has been forced to make an emergency landing in a remote and mountainous area on the return trip to base after transporting a patient. The teammates know the most important resource to conserve is:
rationale: When considering survival situations, flight crews should be aware of the rule of 3's: people can survive for 3 minutes without oxygen, 3 hours without shelter, 3 days without water, and 3 weeks without food. However, the most important resource to conserve is energy, as exhaustion will deplete the hope for survival. For instance, do not waste energy cutting down small trees to build a shelter if the aircraft is intact and can be used as a shelter.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The usual concentration of ketamine is on backorder, so the airway bag is stocked with a different concentration of ketamine than is typically used at a base. The flight paramedic inadvertently administers twice the dose he intended to give the patient. In a system of Just Culture, the fault:
options: Is placed on the paramedic and he should be held accountable for the error
Is shared; the organization should evaluate any processes that may have contributed to the error
Is on the medication supplier for not sending the usual concentration of ketamine
Belongs to no one, this was an honest mistake
rationale: Just Culture is shared accountability in which an organization must look at all contributing factors for the purpose of preventing future errors instead of assigning blame and punishing individuals. Within Just Culture, reckless behavior is punished, but mistakes are met with education and support, along with instituting policies to prevent future errors (ie: place a high alert sticker on a stronger or unusual concentration of ketamine).

stem: The flight crew intubates a patient in acute respiratory distress; the waveform capnography is as follows. What is the priority intervention? [IMAGE DESCRIPTION: Capnography waveform showing ETCO₂ reading of 9 with a flat/near-zero waveform, indicating the ETT may be misplaced (esophageal intubation) or no pulmonary blood flow]
options: Pull the tube and re-attempt the intubation
Place the patient on the mechanical ventilator
rationale: Waveform capnography is the gold standard for endotracheal tube placement. This waveform and low quantitative value indicate that the tube is not in the trachea. Don't yeet your supplies during an intubation - it's poor form to launch your bougie dramatically through the air when pulling it out of the ETT and it does not add razzle dazzle to your intubation. Additionally, you'll wish your bougie was right there on the patient's chest instead of in the wheel well when you need it to fix your goosed tube.

stem: The transport crew arrives at the bedside of a patient on a non-rebreather at 15 liters per minute and vital signs of: HR 70 paced, NIBP 198/114, RR 40, SpO₂ 88%. The patient keeps saying "I can't breathe!" The transport nurse hears crackles on auscultation of lung sounds. The priority intervention is:
rationale: This patient has acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema, as evidenced by hypertension, dyspnea, tachypnea, and hypoxia. To improve gas exchange, this patient needs positive pressure ventilation and these patients often respond quickly to non-invasive ventilation. There are no contraindications to non-invasive present with this patient, so there is no reason to jump to intubation. An anxiolytic is likely to worsen the patient's condition and should not be given. Though this patient may get a dose of diuretics in the course of this illness, improving the respiratory status is the priority.

stem: A post cardiac arrest patient has a pulmonary artery catheter in place with the following readings: CVP: 9, PA: 26/13, CO: 3.9, CI: 1.9, SVR: 1450. What is the most appropriate intervention?
rationale: This patient is in cardiogenic shock, as evidenced by the low cardiac output and cardiac index with high systemic vascular resistance. The right side of the heart (the right atrium and the pulmonary artery pressures) are high because the blood isn't being pushed out of the left side of the heart effectively and it's backing up on the right side. A positive inotrope such as milrinone is going to improve the cardiac output and index by pushing more blood out of the left ventricle with each contraction.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight crew is transporting a patient with a unilateral lung injury. The best position for this patient during transport is:
rationale: When a patient has a unilateral lung injury, the patient should be positioned with the good lung down to resolve the V/Q mismatch. Perfusion follows gravity and when the patient is placed with the good lung (the one being best ventilated) down, the perfusion is directed more toward the good lung. This is why you bend over and put your hands on your knees after you go up a few flights of stairs - your huffing and puffing is adequately ventilating your whole lung, but when standing upright you're mostly perfusing the lower portion of your lungs. Bending over helps pull blood through the full height of the pulmonary vasculature.

stem: The transport nurse is caring for a patient in an acute asthma exacerbation and administers a duo-neb (albuterol and ipratropium). Before the treatment, the patient's lungs were severely diminished and now the patient has significant audible wheezes. The nurse recognizes:
options: The patient requires emergent endotracheal intubation
This likely was not an asthma exacerbation
rationale: The worst lung sounds are no lung sounds, and when an asthma patient has significantly diminished/no lung sounds, they are not moving air. While the end goal will be clear, unobstructed breathing, the progression from a severe asthma exacerbation will include going from near silent lungs to loud wheezes to fine wheezes to finally (hopefully) clear lung sounds with the resolution of symptoms.

stem: The patient with acute pancreatitis will require all of the following EXCEPT:
rationale: Morphine will worsen pancreatitis symptoms and can slow healing due to inducing spasms in the sphincter of Oddi.

stem: The flight team moved a patient from the ICU bed to the flight cot in preparation for transport; the patient underwent a procedure earlier in the day to remove a carotid arterial occlusion. The patient complains of intense pain at the surgical site and the vitals are as follows: Temperature 37.6° C, HR 76, NIBP 172/96, SpO₂ 96%, ETCO₂ 43, RR 21. The crew recognizes:
options: This is normal following the endarterectomy
The need to administer a push dose beta blocker
The need for emergent endotracheal intubation
The need to initiate a nicardipine infusion
rationale: A common complication of endarterectomy (surgery to remove occlusions in arteries, in this case the carotid artery) is rupture of the postoperative artery and subsequent bleeding. There would be rapid hematoma formation and neck swelling, which would indicate the need for emergent airway management before the trachea becomes impeded by adjacent hematoma formation and tissue swelling.

stem: The nurse is preparing to transport a patient with a pulmonary artery catheter in place following the development of cardiogenic shock secondary to an acute myocardial infarction. What is the expected hemodynamic profile of this patient?
options: Hypotension, low CO/CI, low SVR, low PA pressure, low CVP
Hypotension, low CO/CI, high SVR, high PA pressures, high CVP
Hypertension, normal CO/CI, high SVR, high PA pressure, high CVP
Hypotension, low CO/CI, normal SVR, PA pressure, and CVP
rationale: The hemodynamic profile expected in a patient in cardiogenic shock is going to be low cardiac output (normal 4-8) and index (2.5-5) because of poor left ventricular function, which the body will attempt to compensate with increased SVR (800-1200). Additionally, pressures on the right side of the heart will be elevated because the blood isn't being pushed out of the left side of the heart and is backing up into the pulmonary vasculature (leading to pulmonary edema) and right ventricle/atrium.

stem: The patient's serum potassium level is 6.1 mEq/L. The transport crew plans to administer which drug first?
rationale: The primary concern with hyperkalemia is the patient going into a fatal dysrhythmia due to the increased excitability of the cells. While giving calcium will not lower serum potassium levels, it will tell the heart to just chill out for a hot second by stabilizing the cardiac membrane. This priority intervention will buy the crew time to give medications that will lower the serum potassium, most notably insulin. You can get a bit of a potassium shift into the cell using albuterol, but it's not enough to resolve the hyperkalemia entirely.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight crew is unable to ventilate or oxygenate the patient and prepares to perform an emergent surgical cricothyrotomy. The first maneuver should be:
options: Vertical incision extending from cricoid cartilage to thyroid cartilage
Application of passive O₂ for safe apnea period
Insertion of needle through cricothyroid membrane
rationale: The first step in performing an emergent surgical airway is to incise the skin and subcutaneous tissue vertically to expose the cricothyroid membrane, followed by the horizontal incision through the cricothyroid membrane providing access into the trachea. Then, a bougie can be inserted, followed by a 6.0 endotracheal tube, which should be inserted only until the cuff is fully in the trachea. Take extreme care to secure the endotracheal tube, as most of the tube will be exposed and can be easily dislodged during transport.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The transport team is preparing a patient with Pierre Robin Syndrome for transport. They know to:
options: Place the patient in side-lying position
rationale: Pierre Robin syndrome is a cluster of developmental orofacial anomalies that include a cleft palate, a small and recessed chin, and a posterior tongue that often causes airway occlusion. For transport these patients should have an oropharyngeal airway in place and the crew should consider placing the patient prone for gravity to assist with keeping the tongue more anterior.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Interpret the following ABG: pH 7.31, CO₂ 54, HCO₃ 25, PaO₂ 67
rationale: The pH is low, indicating acidosis. The CO2 is high (normal is 35-45) which pushes the pH lower, meaning that it's CO2's fault that the pH is low and thus we have respiratory acidosis. The bicarb is normal, which tells us there is no compensation. The PaO2 is low (normal is 80-100) which adds hypoxemia.

stem: A patient presents to a rural emergency department complaining of chest pain and dizziness. They perform basic labs which are largely unremarkable and a chest x-ray reveals a widened mediastinum. When the flight crew arrives at the bedside, the patient's vitals are: Temperature 37.5, HR 112, NIBP 174/85, RR 24, SpO₂ 97% on room air, and ETCO₂ 29. The crew's priority is:
options: Administer 324mg aspirin and rapid transport to PCI capable facility
Reduce pulse, blood pressure, and treat pain
Place the patient on BiPAP with 100% FiO₂
rationale: A widened mediastinum is seen on a chest x-ray and indicates a thoracic aortic aneurysm or dissection and is a surgical emergency that requires rapid transport to a cardiothoracic surgeon for immediate repair. En route to the OR, the patient should have - in this order - pulse reduction (goal heart rate of 60), blood pressure management (systolic under 120mmHg), and pain management. Think of each systole as a hammer hitting a fragile pane of glass - each swing of the hammer is a heart beat and the strength of the hammer strike is the pressure of the systolic push. Reducing both of those is going to protect whatever fragile integrity the aorta has left in it, and the goal is to buy us enough time to get the patient to the OR. This patient will be intubated in the OR, but that is not the priority at this time.

stem: The critical care transport team is handed a new ABG on the intubated asthma exacerbation patient as they depart the ICU toward the ambulance for the 2 hour transport. The ABG reads: pH 7.11, CO₂ 71, HCO₃ 25, PaO₂ 63, Lactate 3.8. During transport the crew medically manages the patient and titrates the ventilator and the ETCO₂ reads 50. The crew estimates the current arterial pH to be:
rationale: There is an inversely proportional relationship (one goes up, the other goes down) between arterial pH and CO2 with predictable likely changes. For every change in CO2 of 10mmHg, we can expect the pH to change by 0.08 in the opposite direction. Although the first information was ascertained from a blood gas, capnography is an adequate surrogate for arterial carbon dioxide and is typically within 2-3 points lower than actual arterial CO2 due to dilution of dead space air (anatomic or within the vent circuit).

stem: The flight crew sees the chest x-ray of the patient in an asthma exacerbation and identifies hyperinflation. They recognize this is caused by:
rationale: Auto-PEEP is caused by the incomplete exhalation phase, and when repeated, the incoming breath stacks on top of the volume left in by the previous breath. Patients with outflow obstructions, such as asthma or COPD, are not able to fully exhale before the inflow of the next breath, which causes hyperinflation of the lungs. This is visible on chest x-ray by wide costophrenic angles (flattened diaphragm) and longer than baseline lung fields. When this occurs chronically, the bones change shape to accommodate these super full lungs, which is known as barrel chest.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: What factors contribute to the development of fog?
options: Humidity, barometric pressure, and cloud coverage
Air temperature, ground temperature, and humidity
Heat index, humidity, and wind speed
rationale: Fog develops when there is adequate moisture in the air (humidity) and the temperature and dew point approach the same value. Wind can help to prevent the development of fog, but plays a lesser role in fog forecasting than the other three variables. When temperature and dew point are forecasted to be near each other with high humidity and calm winds, it is prudent to expect fog.

stem: The flight nurse is watching a documentary on the soccer team stuck in a cave in Taiwan in the summer of 2018. The young boys ventured into the cave and monsoon rains began while they were in the cave, forcing them deeper into the cave system by rising water. The boys were discovered a week later, and it took another week to safely extricate them. Upon rescue, the boys were found to be mildly hypoxic. What type of hypoxia were they experiencing?
rationale: The 13 boys were stuck in a small air pocket at the uppermost point of the cave system for over 2 weeks before being rescued, and in that time, they consumed much of the oxygen in the ambient air. The room air when the cave divers found them was in the low teens (compared to the normal 21%). Hypoxia caused by a lack of sufficient oxygen is called hypoxic hypoxia, which sounds redundant. The fix for this is to increase the FiO2 (fraction of inspired oxygen). Stagnant hypoxia is insufficient blood flow (cardiac arrest or a shock state), histotoxic hypoxia is a disruption in energy production due to a defect in the cellular usage of oxygen such as cyanide poisoning. And finally, hypemic hypoxia is the reduced oxygen carrying capacity of the hemoglobin, such as sickle cell anemia or carbon monoxide poisoning (don't confuse CO poisoning as a histotoxic hypoxia!).

stem: The Mobile ICU team is 15 minutes out from arriving at a small rural facility to transport a patient back to the large urban burn ICU. Dispatch relays that a family of two adults and seven children all require transport after a house fire started while they were sleeping and currently four are intubated for smoke inhalation injuries. What medication should the crew plan to administer?
rationale: Housefires often cause histotoxic hypoxia secondary to cyanide poisoning due to the burning of synthetic fibers in furniture and mattresses, which requires hydroxocobalamin infusions as the antidote. The providers should anticipate the common side effects of headache, reddened skin, and urine discoloration.

stem: The crew intubated a patient 15 minutes ago and had adequate feedback to confirm correct endotracheal tube placement. The ventilator is now alarming "Low pressure." All of the following are a possible cause EXCEPT:
options: Inadvertent ETT tube advancement to right main stem
rationale: An ETT advancing into the right mainstem will cause HIGH pressure alarms because the ventilator is attempting to deliver an entire breath into a single lung instead of two. The patient overbreathing the vent and pulling their own air will reduce the resistance the ventilator is delivering the breath against and cause low pressure alarms. A low ETT cuff pressure (adults should be 20-30cmH2O) will allow air to escape around the cuff and out the patient's mouth, and a disconnected ventilator circuit will provide no resistance for the vent to push a breath against; both will cause low pressure alarms.

stem: A trauma patient was intubated by the ground crew and when placed on the flight crew's monitor and ventilator using appropriate settings in volume targeted SIMV. The vitals and vent assessment were as follows: HR 104, NIBP 98/61, ETCO₂ 41, SpO₂ 96%, PIP 20, Pplat 13, auto-PEEP 0, frequency matches rate. Ten minutes later, the vitals and ventilator assessment are as follows: HR 106, NIBP 94/62, ETCO₂ 38, SpO₂ 94%, PIP 21, Pplat 19, auto-PEEP 0, frequency matches rate. The crew should:
options: Give Q10 minute medication pushes for sedation and pain control
Disconnect the mechanical ventilator and perform bag-valve ventilations
rationale: In a patient on the mechanical ventilator, an early sign of a pneumothorax is an increase in plateau pressure (Pplat). The Pplat measures alveolar compliance - or the ability of the alveoli to accept the breath given to it. If there is a developing pneumothorax, those alveoli cannot expand to accommodate a delivered breath, and will therefore exhibit high plateau pressure. It's like the breath slams into a brick wall instead of being absorbed by stretchy alveoli. If left untreated, the patient will experience hypotension (due to increasing intrathoracic pressure compressing the vena cava), hypoxia (poor gas exchange), and high inspiratory pressures from the ventilator.

stem: The crew is transporting a 46-year-old male to the Level 1 trauma center 35 minutes by rotor wing after both of his legs were crushed on a construction site by a large cement pipe rolling off a truck. The patient reports that he is generally healthy, he takes no daily medications, and he runs 3-5 miles per day. The crew observes bradycardia and a wide QRS complex and recognizes:
options: That this is an expected finding in a long distance runner
The urgent need to administer calcium gluconate
The need for rapid sequence intubation
rationale: While you may have been tempted to choose the "normal baseline bradycardia because he's a runner" option, you should have been clued in by the red flag in the question about the crush injury. Due to cell lysis in crush-injured patients, there is a high incidence of hyperkalemia, which requires administration of calcium to stabilize the cardiac membrane until you can get the patient to the hospital for definitive care. Remember that potassium is the primary INTRACELLULAR ion, and if you bust open a bunch of cells (cell lysis), they'll spill their potassium out into the vasculature.

stem: The critical care transport team checks the ETT cuff pressure of the 39-year-old female trauma patient and adjusts it to:
rationale: Checking an ETT cuff pressure should be a priority on any intubated patient because of the harm an over- or under-inflated cuff can cause. For adults, the recommendation is 20-30cmH2O (peds = 15-20cmH2O).

stem: The most common electrolyte disturbance is:
rationale: Sodium is the body's most abundant extracellular ion and is the most frequently deranged lab value. Severity of symptoms are proportional to level of deviation and how quickly the sodium levels changed. A patient whose sodium has been falling slowly over a few weeks is going to have more subtle neuro changes than someone who had sudden development of dilutional hyponatremia from drinking 3 gallons of water. Sodium levels should be corrected slowly, but with consideration to how quickly they were pushed out of normal range.

stem: The flight crew is transporting a patient to a hospital with high risk OB services for management of preeclampsia. She was given a 4 gram magnesium bolus at the sending facility and is on a 4 gram per hour magnesium infusion. During the transport, the patient falls asleep, her respiratory rate falls from 22 to 14, and she requires nail bed pressure to wake her up. The crew:
options: Understands that the patient is likely exhausted and the cabin lights should be lowered so she can rest
Administers an appropriate dose of a benzodiazepine
Stops the magnesium infusion and administers calcium gluconate
rationale: Signs of magnesium toxicity are decreased deep tendon reflexes (very common test question, but difficult to assess in the cramped spaces of a small airframe), respiratory depression, and altered mental status. It's imperative to monitor capnography for a close watch of respiratory rate (and the capnography will significantly increase due to bradypnea) and stop the magnesium infusion. The treatment for magnesium toxicity is calcium administration.

stem: The crew is transporting a patient with a potassium level of 6.7, as indicated by the portable blood analysis system (commonly known as epoc or I-STAT). The most appropriate prehospital treatment is:
options: Calcium administration and begin the patient on continuous nebulized albuterol
A dextrose bolus followed by 20 units of IV regular insulin
rationale: This hyperkalemic patient (normal serum potassium 3.5-5.0) is at a high risk for fatal cardiac dysrhythmias and should be given calcium to stabilize the cardiac membrane, followed by a continuous nebulized albuterol treatment to begin shifting the serum potassium into the cell. There will be a minimal decrease in the potassium level, but it can at least prevent the hyperkalemia from worsening during transport. The definitive treatment will be dialysis to remove the potassium, but once in hospital, the patient can be given a dextrose bolus (to prevent hypoglycemia) and insulin to significantly shift the potassium into the cell. Administering an insulin bolus is not available to the vast majority of prehospital agencies, and 20 units would be a very high dose. Additionally, it is prudent to place hyperkalemic patients on defibrillation pads in the event they experience a shockable rhythm.

stem: An electrical lineman is being managed in the burn ICU after an electrocution injury. The nurse tracks his urine output and finds that it is 55mL/hour and tea colored. The nurse recognizes:
options: That this is adequate urine output
The patient is likely in fluid overload and IV fluids should be slowed
The need to increase IV fluid infusion rate
rationale: While the standard hourly urine output is 30mL/hour or the weight based standard of 0.5ml/kg/hr are both met here, this patient has an electrical burn and requires renal protective fluid administration, which is titrated to urine output of 100mL/hr. This patient requires a higher IV fluid rate in order to achieve renal protective urine output.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A 39-year-old male patient tells the nurse that he took an entire bottle of aspirin 4 hours ago. The nurse suspects:
rationale: Initially, the patient with salicylate toxicity will present with respiratory alkalosis due to the aspirin directly stimulating the cerebral medulla, but that is short lived as the salicylate causes uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation, thus inducing severe metabolic acidosis. These patients will have significantly elevated lactate levels and a high anion gap. Treatment includes fluid resuscitation to correct shock, adequate ventilatory support to compensate for acidosis, dialysis to reduce serum salicylate levels, and symptom reduction (benzodiazepines for seizures, etc.). Reye's syndrome is a rare complication caused by giving pediatric patients aspirin; SJS and exophthalmos have no correlation with aspirin toxicity.

stem: The flight paramedic would anticipate the patient with decompensated septic shock to have which hemodynamic profile?
options: Low CVP, cardiac output and index, high SVR
Low CVP, SVR, cardiac output and index
Normal CVP, low cardiac index and output, normal SVR
Normal CVP, high cardiac output, low cardiac index, low SVR
rationale: The patient in decompensated shock has lost the peripheral clamp down that is often present in early septic shock and now has low systemic vascular resistance (SVR, normal 800-1200), along with poor perfusion as evidenced by low cardiac output (CO, normal 4-8Lpm), cardiac index (CI, normal 2.5-4.0), and central venous pressure (CVP, or "preload" normal 2-8).

stem: A patient calls 911 and tells the paramedic she took 20 tabs of extra strength Tylenol (500mg per tab) about 5 hours ago to hurt herself, but she now regrets it and wants help. She denies any pain, her vital signs are normal, and her point of care glucose is 94. The paramedic understands:
options: This patient has metabolized most of the medication and requires no medical attention
Acetaminophen is a relatively harmless medication, the paramedic should encourage adequate PO intake and ascertain a refusal
This patient is critically ill and requires emergent transport to the ED
This patient should be mechanically restrained for transport for her safety and for the paramedic's safety due to the suicidal ideations
rationale: Acetaminophen toxicity is largely asymptomatic in the first 24 hours, but it is imperative to get the antidote (n-acetylcysteine, also known as NAC and Mucomyst) as quickly as possible. Permanent liver damage begins within 8 hours of ingestion if the antidote is not administered. The progression of acetaminophen toxicity is a slow but miserable progression with a fork in the road to resolution of symptoms or death within 4 days. Prehospital management includes supportive treatment (ABCs) and history gathering (time of ingestion, amount, specific formulary). Acetaminophen is part of many OTC cocktails, like migraine medications, menstrual symptom treatment, cold and flu medications, and pain meds. Don't underestimate the dangers of an acetaminophen overdose!

stem: The flight crew is at the bedside of a patient with septic shock and an arterial line in place to optimize titration of multiple pressors. The crew knows:
options: Due to changes associated with flight physiology, it is not recommended to fly patients with an arterial line
To give the patient a 500mL bolus prior to switching between the hospital monitor and the transport monitor
To zero the art line after connecting to the transport monitor, at altitude, and upon landing
rationale: Patients with an arterial line should have the line zeroed when switching to a different monitor and with any significant atmospheric pressure change (ascent, descent). If there is suspicion for inaccuracy (over- or underdampening), the clinician should zero the art line and/or perform a fast flush test. On a fast flush, there should be three oscillations; more than three means it's hyperdynamic with underdampening, less indicates overdampening.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The brain stem sits in the bottom opening of the skull, known as the
rationale: This is a simple information recall question, and you likely won't see many of them on your advanced certification exam. However, it is important to know these tidbits and how to apply them to patient care concepts. It's easy to mix up foramen magnum (the opening on the bottom side of the skull) and the foramen of Monro (connection between the ventricles in the brain) because they are both structures in the noggin and they both start with M. While rote memorization of information tends to be painstaking and unreliable for recall, sometimes it is the only option. In regard to nomenclature of anatomy, rote memorization is the only option.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A 5-year-old patient that weighs 44 pounds should produce urine output at a rate of at least:
rationale: Weight based urine output should be 0.5mL/kg/hour for adults, 1mL/kg/hour for pediatrics, and 1.5mL/kg/hour for infants. For this patient, 44 pounds = 20kg, and 20kg x 1ml = 20mL/hour minimum urine output. Just as weight based urine output is 0.5/1.0/1.5 for adults/pediatrics/infants, minute ventilation has a similar pattern of 100/150/200mL/kg of ideal body weight for adults/pediatrics/infants. Use that as a tool to help guide you and think of the reasons for those patterns.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: According to the Code of Federal Regulations, daytime weather minimums for conducting VFR flights in non-mountainous local flying area are:
rationale: The federal weather minimums are 800 and 2 for non-mountainous daytime flying for VFR flights. There are various weather minimums respective to topography and flight distance and can be found in the Code of Federal Regulations 135.609.

stem: A paramedic inadvertently administers the wrong dose of a medication after typing in the pediatric patient's weight wrong on the duty phone app. According to Just Culture:
options: The paramedic should be educated on the proper use of the pediatric medication application
The paramedic should be placed on a performance improvement plan
Nothing should be done, this was a human error
rationale: Just Culture states that mistakes not caused by reckless behavior or by being careless should be supported. We are all human beings and are subject to honest mistakes. When corners are being cut, the clinician should be trained or educated on the importance of always choosing the right course of action. When errors are made due to recklessness, the offender should be held accountable and face punitive action.

stem: The critical care paramedic understands that administering milrinone (Primacor) to the patient in acute decompensated heart failure will:
rationale: Milrinone is a positive inotrope that increases contractility (gives the left ventricle more oomph with each squeeze) and a vasodilator, which lowers systemic vascular resistance and thus reduces afterload (the pressure the left ventricle has to push against).

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Optimizing team dynamics among the pilot, flight paramedic, and flight nurse is achieved by:
options: Creating a hierarchy based on seniority
Dividing and delegating tasks to maximize effective and safe operations
Maintaining a power dynamic such that each team member has equal input in all decisions made about flight and medical operations
Allowing the pilot in command to make all decisions regarding flight operations
rationale: Each member of the team should bear a portion of the responsibility of maintaining safe and effective operations. For example, the pilot in command is ultimately responsible for decisions regarding flights, but the medical crew may decline a flight. Medical crew are responsible for maintaining sterile cockpit during critical phases of flight, and should be on the lookout for other aircraft in controlled airspace. The crew should function well together and know their roles in optimizing the team, and this concept is known as crew resource management.

stem: The flight crew is transporting an intubated and mechanically ventilated patient with an extraventricular drain following a traumatic brain injury sustained in an unhelmeted motorcycle collision. The sending neuro-ICU nurse tells the crew the patient's ICP has been 10--12 for the last 24 hours. After loading the patient into the aircraft, the medic notices the monitor displays a reading of 29. What is a likely explanation?
options: The patient's head is turned to the side
rationale: Head positioning has a significant impact on intracranial pressure (ICP) and a simple turning of the patient's head can cause a big increase in ICP. Within transport, we are constantly moving our patients and it would be easy for the patient's head to fall to one side while loading the patient in the aircraft. The most likely of these options would be the patient's head position. Outside of test taking, checking head position is a fast, easy way to identify or rule out a change in ICP. While you might cleverly try to keep a patient's head positioned midline with a c-collar for transport, this tactic should not be employed because neck compression by the c-collar can impede venous outflow and increase the ICP.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The helicopter experienced a hard landing as it approached their home helipad and a sudden gust of wind hit the aircraft. The skids collapsed under the helicopter and the aircraft was flush with the ground. The priority is to:
options: Immediately disembark the aircraft and meet at the 12 o'clock position
Complete the shutdown sequence and remain in the aircraft until the blades stop turning
rationale: As long as there is no reason to immediately leave the aircraft (ie: smoke or fire in the aircraft), the crew should remain inside the aircraft and complete the shutdown sequence (throttle, fuel, battery, rotor brake). Once the blades have stopped turning, the crew should get out and meet at the 12 o'clock position, then assess for injuries and seek help.

stem: The EKG of a hemodynamically unstable patient reveals electrical alternans. What is the priority intervention?
rationale: Electrical alternans occurs in cardiac tamponade when pericardial sac fills with fluid (usually due to malignancy, but can also occur secondary to trauma) and the heart ding-dongs back and forth inside the liquid-filled sac. It also prevents the heart from expanding to fill with blood during diastole and the patient will also exhibit Beck's Triad, a trio of symptoms that includes muffled heart tones (because the sound is having to travel through the fluid), jugular vein distention (because the blood is backing up the right side of the heart and into the next biggest/closest veins), and narrowed pulse pressure (because the heart cannot expand and fill during diastole, and due to Starling's Law it doesn't have the sling shot momentum to push much blood forward). The treatment is to evacuate the pericardial sac to relieve the tamponade that is smashing in on the heart from all sides.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: There is a slow leak in the cabin pressure of a fixed wing aircraft and the captain is trying to swat his hat at a lizard on the windscreen, and in reality there is no lizard on the windscreen. The copilot recognizes the captain is in what stage of hypoxia?
rationale: There are four stages of hypoxia. Indifferent: few physiologic changes, may include mild tachycardia and increased minute ventilation. Compensatory: the body compensates with increased cardiac output and increased minute ventilation, but is heavily influenced by the individual's physical condition. Disturbance: compensatory mechanisms are no longer capable of counteracting the severity of the hypoxia and the individual experiences behavioral, psychomotor, and personality changes. Critical: Judgment and coordination are impaired and the individual will become incapacitated within minutes.

stem: The crew is transferring a critically ill patient with acetaminophen toxicity from an intentional ingestion 10 hours ago. The crew anticipates:
rationale: The mainstay of acetaminophen toxicity reversal is n-acetylcysteine (NAC, Mucomyst) and it must be started within 8 hours of the ingestion and continues until serum acetaminophen levels fall to zero, typically about 24 hours. Not-so-fun-fact: it's not the acetaminophen that damages the liver, it's the toxic metabolite (the byproduct of its digestion in the body) called oxoproline.

stem: Beck's triad
options: Is a cluster of signs indicating cardiac tamponade
Is the injury pattern seen in pediatric trauma when struck by a vehicle
rationale: Beck's triad is a cluster of three signs seen in the presence of cardiac tamponade: muffled heart tones (the heart's sounds are being drowned out by the surrounding fluid), jugular vein distention (blood can't get pushed out of the heart and is backing up into the adjacent big veins), and narrowing pulse pressure (the heart cannot expand and fill with blood during diastole, thus the contraction of the heart doesn't push much blood out).

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A hiker that lives on the east coast travels to climb Mount Rainier and feels short of breath, is fatigued, and must take frequent breaks on his hike. This is explained by
options: Atmospheric oxygen concentration of 15% at that altitude
rationale: Atmospheric oxygen is 21% at sea level and at the top of Mount Rainier, but unacclimated climbers will become hypoxic because the oxygen molecules are much larger at altitude due to the decrease in atmospheric pressure. At sea level, the individual could draw in a bazillion molecules of oxygen, but at altitude only a handful of molecules fit into the climber's lungs. Additionally, the partial pressure of the oxygen is less, thus less is dissolved into the patient's blood.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight crew rendezvouses with an ALS crew for a patient with altered mental status. Upon entering the ambulance, the flight crew finds the patient shirtless with denim coveralls on and notices caput medusae across the patient's abdomen. The crew prioritizes
rationale: Caput medusae is Latin for "head of Medusa" and describes the Medusa-like appearance of the engorged veins that sprawl across the patient's abdomen in advanced liver disease due to prolonged portal hypertension. This should be an immediate red flag for the clinician to anticipate other dangers associated with liver failure such as esophageal varices (prevent nausea and vomiting which can cause esophageal rupture), bleeding (liver patients often present with coagulopathy), and hepatic encephalopathy (due to high ammonia levels).

stem: The flight paramedic checks her own oxygen saturation on the dead leg home out of curiosity. She finds that her pulse oximetry is 87% while flying at 4,500 feet AGL. What type of hypoxia is she experiencing?
rationale: The oxygen molecules are larger at altitude and the partial pressure is less, thus less oxygen will diffuse across the alveolar capillary membrane, which is hypoxic hypoxia (lack of oxygen available for gas exchange).

stem: A patient has been in status epilepticus for 19 minutes. The crew has administered three doses of benzodiazepines, assessed blood glucose, and inserted a supraglottic airway. The next most appropriate action is to
options: Initiate seizure precautions to protect the patient from injury
rationale: This patient needs an antiepileptic agent, such as levetiracetam (Keppra). Chemically paralyzing this patient would stop the physical movement caused by the seizure, but would not stop the seizure itself. Seizure precautions should have already been initiated to protect this patient 19 minutes into the seizure. The patient's airway is being managed with a supraglottic airway according to the information given in the question. Do not let suggestions in the answer choices cause you to imagine information or details that are not given in the question.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The helicopter is flying at 3000 feet AGL. The temperature is 26° C on the ground but at cruise altitude it is _____.
rationale: The standard relationship between altitude and temperature is a 2 degrees C decrease for every thousand feet of elevation. Crews can use this to their advantage, for instance if the air conditioner is non functioning in the aircraft and the patient can tolerate the pressure change, the pilot can climb higher than normal to reach cooler temperatures.

stem: Waddell’s Triad includes ________ and describes ________.
options: Rib fractures, pulmonary contusion, and aortic injuries - lateral impact injury patterns
Ipsilateral femur fracture and intra-thoracic or abdominal injury, and contralateral head injury - pediatric pedestrian versus car injury pattern
Muffled heart tones, JVD, and narrowing pulse pressures - signs of cardiac tamponade
Fever, tachycardia, and tachypnea - early signs of sepsis
rationale: Waddell's Triad is a group of three injuries commonly seen in pediatric patients that are struck by vehicles and occur because of the child's size in proportion to the vehicle. The femoral shaft fracture occurs because the bumper strikes the child at the level of the femur (ipsilateral because the injury occurs on the same side that the child is struck on), intra-abdominal and thoracic injuries because of the body contacting the hood of the car, and contralateral (opposite-sided) head injuries from the child being thrown off the car and landing on the opposite side of their head. The flight clinician should assess pediatric patients and expect these injuries in the mechanism of being struck by a car.

stem: The single best way to confirm endotracheal tube placement is
options: Absence of gastric sounds and presence of bilateral lung sounds
rationale: All endotracheal tube placements should be confirmed using quantitative and qualitative (numeric and waveform) capnography. People who come from a prehospital background are accustomed to placing all critically ill patients on capnography, but in-hospital practice is a bit behind the times on this one and nurses tend to want to confirm ET tube placements with a portable chest film. DON'T FALL FOR IT!

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The patient is currently being ventilated with 60% FiO₂ to maintain normoxia and is being transported by rotor wing across mountainous terrain that will require a 6,000 foot ascent. The atmospheric pressure on the ground is 700mmHg and the pressure at altitude will be 550mmHg. What FiO₂ will the patient require at altitude?
rationale: Using the Adjusted FiO2 Calculator will guide you in knowing how to compensate with oxygen concentration for how altitude impacts our patients. This concept comes from Henry's Law that explains the pressure applied to a gas is directly proportional to the amount of gas that will dissolve into the liquid. In this application, we are trying to dissolve oxygen into the patient's blood and when we go to altitude, we have less atmospheric pressure and will, therefore, dissolve less oxygen into the blood. We mitigate this by increasing the concentration of oxygen delivered to the blood. The formula is: FiO2 (ground) x mmHg (ground) = FiO2 (altitude) x mmHg (altitude). For example: 60% x 700mmHg = FiO2 x 550mmHg, solving to 76.4%.

stem: The intubated patient has a RASS score of +1 before leaving the ER room at the sending facility for the rotor transport to higher care. The flight crew recognizes
options: The need to reverse the chemical sedation given by the sending facility
The need to chemically paralyze the patient prior to loading the patient in the aircraft
The need to administer a dose of analgesic
The need to administer additional sedation
rationale: Transporting a patient by rotor wing aircraft introduces noxious stimuli, including the loud engines and rotor noise, the smell of Jet A on start up, vibrations, and the racket of moving them from an ER cot to the flight cot and into the aircraft. While a RASS of +1 is appropriate in a quiet ICU or even a controlled emergency department, that patient is likely going to exhibit a physical response to the helicopter experience. These stimuli can present with agitation (which increases cerebral oxygen demand), increased pulse and blood pressure, attempted extubation, etc. Sedated patients still feel pain and should be receiving analgesics to address pain, but this question was specifically asking about the patient's level of sedation.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Most air medical crashes are caused by
rationale: The FAA describes poor judgment in reference to causative factors to aviation crashes as a series of mistakes that may lead to an accident or incident. Two basic principles generally associated with the creation of a poor judgment chain are: 1) one bad decision often leads to another; and 2) as a string of bad decisions grows, it reduces the number of subsequent alternatives for continued safe flight. Aeronautical decision-making is intended to break the poor judgment chain before it can cause an accident or incident.

stem: The transport team is assisting ventilations to preoxygenate a patient in preparation to intubate the patient. While placing the commercial tube securement device under the patient's neck, the flight nurse palpates crepitus. The flight nurse suspects
rationale: A tracheobronchial injury will allow air to escape during ventilations (worsened with positive pressure ventilation) and can cause pockets of subcutaneous emphysema that can be identified by palpation. The test answer treatment is to insert the endotracheal tube beyond the point of the injury to ventilate distal to the disruption, which includes intentionally right main stemming the patient. However, in real life these airways can be exceptionally difficult to intubate and the clinician should be prepared to perform a surgical cricothyrotomy. Additionally, it takes an insanely high amount of force to cause this injury and these patients often have concomitant catastrophic injuries and thus, a high morbidity and mortality.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The fixed wing crew is preparing to fly a patient cross country and the pilot briefs the medical crew that they will be flying at 40,000 feet MSL. The medical crew knows that at this altitude if there is a loss of cabin pressure they will have _____ of useful consciousness.
rationale: A loss of cabin pressure at 40,000 feet would leave the crew only 10 seconds to apply oxygen to themselves before they lose the ability to make corrections. Be aware that a slow leak of cabin pressure (eg: a crack in the window) will give the crew more time than if there is a sudden loss of pressure (eg: if a passenger becomes combative and rips open the cabin door).

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The rotor crew is called to the scene of a patient in respiratory distress after exposure to noxious gas while at work. The priority is to
options: Decontaminate the patient before loading him into the aircraft
Obtain permission from ICS to transport the patient
Wrap the patient in mylar blanket prior to transport
rationale: All patients with a hazardous material exposure and contamination should be decontaminated before loading them into the aircraft. While the patient should be cared for (including airway management), the priority is to decontaminate them so that the clinicians do not fall prey to the ill effects of the contaminant. Afterall, the crew can't care for the patient if they are unconscious from inhaling toxic fumes that the patient was off-gassing.

stem: The critical care transport crew arrives at a small emergency department to transport a 27-year-old woman that delivered a full term neonate 20 minutes ago. The sending physician tells you that during delivery of the placenta, he had to pull traction on the umbilical cord to facilitate delivery; the placenta is in a specimen tub at the bedside and the transport nurse notices that there is a significant portion of the placenta missing. During rapid transport to the receiving facility, the transport team plans to:
