rationale: Rhabdomyolysis is the breakdown of a large amount of muscle that releases heaps of myoglobin into the bloodstream, overwhelming the kidneys and in severe cases, can shut the kidneys down altogether. Patients will present with intense pain to the affected tissues, oliguria, very dark urine, edema, nausea and vomiting, and hyperkalemia because of the widespread cell lysis. Be on the lookout for this in patients with crush injuries, burns more than 24 hours past the time of injury, elderly patients that fell and laid there for 2 days before being found, and people that didn't know their own limits when they tried CrossFit for the first time.

stem: The flight nurse anticipates _____ in the patient diagnosed with diabetes insipidus.
options: Low serum osmolality, high urine osmolality
High serum osmolality, high urine osmolality
High serum osmolality, low urine osmolality
Low serum osmolality, low urine osmolality
rationale: Diabetes insipidus is a condition in which there is a lack of antidiuretic hormone (ADH), or that the kidneys stop responding to the ADH. Break down ADH: ANTI: not doing this; DIURETIC: creating urine; HORMONE: chemical messenger. If the patient doesn't have any anti-urinate-messenger, they will over diurese and dump all their plasma volume out and hemoconcentrate their intravascular volume, resulting in high serum osmolality and very low (think straight up water) urine osmolality. This can be caused by TBI, so watch for buckets of urine output in your head trauma patients. You can give exogenous ADH, which you and I know as vasopressin.

stem: When assessing ETT depth on the recently intubated patient, the transport nurse confirms adequate depth
options: At the level of the carina
At the level of the cricothyroid cartilage
rationale: Don't confuse this with ETT placement - the gold standard for placement is waveform capnography, but for depth we use an AP CXR (anterior-posterior view chest radiograph). The distal tip of the ETT should sit approximately 5 centimeters above the carina, give or take 2 centimeters. An easy-to-visualize quick cheat code is to look for where the collar bones meet the sternum and look for the distal tip of the ETT to sit a bit below that. Anything higher risks displacement, and oppositely, anything at or below the level of the carina risks right main stemming the tube.

stem: The full term pregnant patient was the victim of assault from her domestic partner and her only complaint is severe abdominal pain. There is no bleeding from the vagina and the mother is hemodynamically stable. The transport team
options: Obtains a refusal and refers the woman to her OB
Administers 50mcg fentanyl, as NSAIDs are contraindicated in late pregnancy
rationale: Placental abruption presents with painful bright red bleeding, but the bleeding can be contained within the uterus if the fetus is head down, engaged with the cervix. The fetal head acts as a cork and holds that blood inside, but momma is still sick. Pregnant patients will not show signs of hemodynamic instability (like a high shock index) until late in the progression of the hemorrhage, so be ready with blood products and a Jet A bolus before her pressure bottoms out. Abruptio placentae has a high fetal and maternal mortality rate. When caring for the pregnant patient, remember that the standard SpO2 allowance of 92% and up does not apply! Keep these women at an SpO2 of 95% or higher.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Interpret the following arterial blood gas: pH 7.35, CO₂ 59, HCO₃ 30
rationale: The pH is within normal limits, but it's on the acidotic end of normal. The CO2 is high, which would influence the pH toward acidosis, but the bicarb is also high (remember the bicarb is a base so if it's high, it's pushing the pH up toward alkalosis). What we have is a balanced game of tug-of-war, but the CO2 has a leg up on bicarb. The bicarb is fully compensating for the CO2 and the pH is fully restored to normal (7.35-7.45). This is fully compensated respiratory acidosis; this might be a COPD patient, they are always retaining CO2 and it's been going on long enough that their metabolic system sees it and compensates.

stem: The transport team is caring for a patient with an LVAD. The patient is pulseless, unresponsive, and the crew cannot find a MAP using the doppler. The next step is to:
options: Check battery status of the VAD controller
rationale: The priority is to begin CPR just like you would on any patient. CPR in Ventricular Assist Devices (VADs) used to be contraindicated due to the fear of dislodging the VAD and blood letting the patient's entire volume into their thoracic cavity, however, in 2017 the American Heart Association changed the recommendation. The limited data available shows a better chance of survival when CPR is performed than when CPR was withheld.

stem: The most harmful IABP timing error is
rationale: The purpose of the intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) is to: 1) Improve coronary perfusion (achieved through diastolic balloon inflation which shoves blood backwards back toward the ascending aorta where the coronary vessels branch off), and 2) Reduce the workload on the left side of the heart (achieved through end diastolic balloon deflation, just as the aorta is opening and the left ventricle starts to squeeze, the deflation generates a bit of negative pressure to help suck the blood out of the left ventricle). The IABP does not provide forward flow like an Impella or VAD do. When the balloon stays inflated too long, instead of pulling the piano forward, the balloon just lays its obstructive self on the piano and makes the work of the left ventricle even more difficult.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: When notifying the next of kin of an on-scene death, the clinician should
options: Withhold details of the cause of death
Keep the family members from seeing the body
Use clear language like "died" instead of "passed away"
Leave as soon as safely possible to allow the family to begin grieving
rationale: When delivering difficult news, it is imperative to use plain, clear language. Use your critical thinking skills and compassion to handle the situation with cultural competence to best meet the family's needs (some weep loudly and fall to the floor, some remain composed, and others may vehemently deny the reality of the situation or need you to repeat the statement multiple times). Literature demonstrates over and over that the first few moments of a death notification hold the power to guide the survivor's grief process. Something as simple as offering a tissue, or helping them to the ground when their knees buckle and just sitting in silence with them for a few moments can grant permission to begin grieving. These acts of compassionate human connection help you to process the trauma and prevent cumulative trauma from haunting you well into your retirement from EMS.

stem: The diagnostic difference between diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and hyperosmolar hyperglycemic nonketotic state (HHNK) is:
options: The severity of blood glucose elevation
rationale: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and hyperosmolar hyperglycemic nonketotic state (HHNK) present very similarly, but they are different pathologies. DKA mostly occurs in type 1 diabetics and HHNK mostly happens in type 2 diabetics. The glucose tends to be way higher in HHNK (800+) than in DKA (250+). DKA creates a moderate to severely acidotic state and HHNK typically is not acidotic (but it can present with mild to moderate acidosis). However, the differentiating diagnostic factor is the presence of ketones. It's right there in the name, goofball: diabetic ketoacidosis, and hyperosmolar hyperglycemic nonketotic state. Thankfully, the prehospital management is the same: these patients are profoundly dehydrated and require loads of IV fluids and antiemetics. STEP AWAY FROM THE LARYNGOSCOPE — do not intubate these patients unless you absolutely, positively must. You can trial the huffing and puffing DKA patient on bipap to ease the work of breathing, but allow them to maintain whatever minute ventilation their tiny medulla desires. They need to offload CO₂ to help achieve homeostasis with their rock bottom HCO₃ so don't take that ability away from them by putting them on the mechanical ventilator.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Where should the transducer be leveled for the arterial line?
options: Fifth intercostal space, anterior axillary line
Fourth intercostal space, anterior axillary line
rationale: The arterial line transducer should be leveled at the right atrium of the heart, but because you can't see where that is, we use the phlebostatic axis, which is at the fourth intercostal space, midaxillary line. Don't get mixed up with where we place a chest tube, which is the fourth to fifth intercostal space, anterior axillary line. The axillary space is the landing strip going straight down from the axilla (armpit). The anterior is the front-most line of the armpit region, whereas midaxillary is the smack dab center of the armpit zone.

stem: The patient's plateau pressure is 22 and the PEEP is 5. The flight paramedic recognizes that the driving pressure is:
rationale: Driving pressure is the pressure gradient experienced by the alveoli. It is the difference between the plateau pressure (max alveolar pressure) and PEEP (minimum alveolar pressure): Driving Pressure = Pplat - PEEP. In this case: 22 - 5 = 17. Why it matters: the alveoli are delicate single cell thick sacks that are inflated and deflated with each breath. A high driving pressure (high Pplat with a low PEEP) is going to whip those alveoli back and forth between the high pressure required to push that breath into the lower airways and the end exhalation pressure, and anything above 14 is associated with higher morbidity and more ICU days. To fix a high driving pressure, either reduce the Pplat (give smaller breaths in volume mode or reduce PIP in pressure mode) or increase the PEEP. The worst offenders of high driving pressures are patients with ARDS, which is exactly why we ventilate them with small tidal volumes and high PEEP.

stem: The flight nurse understands that auto-PEEP is
options: Assessed by performing an inspiratory hold
A common occurrence in patients with ARDS
rationale: Auto-PEEP is an assessment performed by pausing the ventilatory cycle at the end of exhalation (e-hold) and measuring the pressure left in the alveoli. If the patient is offloading the breath appropriately, the pressure left in the lungs at the end of exhalation is equal to the PEEP that the ventilator is delivering. However, when patients are breath stacking, or failing to get rid of the inhaled tidal volume, it generates pressure called auto-PEEP. When you see your patients doing this, you have a few options: give them more time to exhale (extend e-time or reduce the respiratory rate) or drop your ventilator delivered pressure by whatever the difference is between your set PEEP and the patient's auto-PEEP. Remember that PEEP is a setting and auto-PEEP is an assessment.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Are you capable of passing your advanced certification exam?
rationale: A successful career in critical care is a choose your own adventure kind of deal; you'll get out of it what you put into it. If you study adequately, using multiple resources, and start applying to your clinical practice what you're learning from the books, you'll do great! Be coachable, be willing to listen, and be open to the idea that you might have been wrong in the past. Put in the elbow grease, and you'll be reaching your career goals in due time!

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight nurse is transporting a patient with an intrarenal acute kidney injury. A finding consistent with this diagnosis is
rationale: A urine osmolality of 1500 mOsm/kg is elevated outside the normal range and is indicative of volume excess and intrarenal acute kidney injury (AKI). In intrarenal AKI, this would lead to volume overload, dry mucous membranes are seen with volume deficit. Intrarenal kidney injury would lead to decreased urine output. A blood urea nitrogen (BUN) of 15 mg/dL is a normal value.

stem: A patient who is 34 weeks pregnant was diagnosed with preeclampsia after presenting with proteinuria and a blood pressure of 170/100 mmHg. She was treated with labetalol (Trandate) and a magnesium sulfate infusion before transport. During the transfer, vital signs were recorded as follows: HR 95 beats/min BP 150/100 mmHg RR 24 breaths/min O2 saturation 94% (room air) Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score 15 Which of the following interventions is indicated?
rationale: Although this preeclamptic patient's blood pressure is elevated, it is not above the treatment threshold of systolic blood pressure ≥160 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure ≥110 mmHg. A therapeutic intervention for patients with preeclampsia receiving magnesium is to administer oxygen to maintain oxygen saturation above 95%.

stem: A patient with an intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) is being transported via fixed-wing aircraft. After ascent, it is essential for the transport nurse to
options: Check for a hematoma at the arterial site
rationale: Rezeroing the arterial line is essential. Checking for a hematoma at the insertion site and reassessing distal pulses are components of the ongoing assessment of a balloon pump. They are not specific to altitude changes. These should be assessed at regular intervals and after the patient is moved. For example, after loading and unloading. Adjusting the timing is not affected by altitude. Timing would only be adjusted if there was an issue. Pressure measure lines will be affected by changes in barometric pressure and should always be rezeroed after ascent and descent or after pressurization and depressurization of an aircraft.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A child is being transferred due to a head injury following an impact with a car. During the transport, the child becomes anxious and tachycardic. The nurse should anticipate this is due to
options: Use of anxiety of the child
rationale: The nurse should anticipate other sources of bleeding. Hypotension is rarely seen in isolated head injuries. When hypotension is seen in multi-system trauma patient, hypotension/hemorrhage should always be ruled out and treated. Hypotension is most often a result of secondary acute hemorrhage from: impaired autonomic nervous system control, iatrogenically from medication use, or less commonly from spinal cord injury or acute diabetes insipidus. If euvolemia does not correct hypotension, infusions of norepinephrine or phenylephrine should be initiated to maintain adequate mean arterial pressure and cerebral perfusion pressure. Anxiousness and tachycardia are natural responses of a child in this situation. However, the critical nurse should have a deeper understanding and suspicion of hypovolemia related to secondary acute hemorrhage.

stem: The flight team is called to transport an intubated child who was found in a house fire. The child has suffered significant burns to their chest, back, bilateral arms, neck, and face. The child has been difficult to ventilate with limited chest wall movement. Ventilator settings: Pressure Control Ventilation FiO2: 100% PEEP: 5 cm H2O Pressure control: 10 cm H2O Respirations: 20 breaths/min Peak inspiratory pressure: 45 cm H2O Vitals signs: BP 95/55 mmHg HR 125 beats/min RR 20 breaths/min O2 Sat: 85% (ventilated) The MOST appropriate intervention is:
rationale: Circumferential burns to the chest wall can cause eschar tissue that will impede chest wall movement. Escharotomy will allow for movement of the chest wall and will improve ventilation. Increasing PEEP with increasing peak inspiratory pressures. Suctioning the airway may improve ventilation, but is not a definitive fix for poor chest movement. Performing a needle decompression will not improve chest wall movement.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight nurse responds to a patient who sustained first-degree frostbite to their feet. The patient's feet are hyperemic and swollen. The sending facility has started rewarming the patient's feet, which will be continued in transport with passive rewarming. Which assessment findings would the flight nurse expect the patient to develop as passive rewarming continues?
options: Decreased pain and redness with increased edema
Increased pain and cyanosis, mottled skin
rationale: As the rewarming of areas of first-degree frostbite, the tissue becomes mottled, cyanotic and painful due to superficial thawing of tissues. During the rewarming of first-degree frostbite, skin becomes deep red, hot and dry to touch. First-degree frostbite does not develop blisters.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight nurse has had a dental procedure to fill a cavity. The nurse should wait how long before returning to work?
rationale: The dental pulp of a tooth can cause severe dental pain called barotrauma. Waiting 48-72 hours after invasive procedures will allow enough time for the tooth to equalize or prevent pain. The other options are either too much or too little time.

stem: The flight nurse is transporting a patient with thoracic trauma. The nurse should recognize that a late sign of hypoxia in this patient is
rationale: Cyanosis is a late finding of hypoxia due to thoracic trauma, and its absence is not a reliable indicator of adequate oxygenation. Low SaO2 is the defining characteristic of hypoxia. Rapid respiratory rate and accessory muscle use are early signs of hypoxia.

stem: Three air ambulances have been called to the scene of a motor vehicle collision involving a tour bus and a tanker carrying diesel fuel. First responders have activated the Incident Command System (ICS). Once on scene, the flight teams can anticipate that they will MOST likely be assigned to which ICS section?
rationale: In large-scale incidents, transport teams generally will be assigned to the Operations section. Logistics supports the Operations group by providing additional resources such as equipment, personnel, and more.

stem: A patient receiving mechanical ventilation is noted to have an increased respiratory rate after engine start. The flight nurse recognizes the PRIORITY intervention is to administer
rationale: When the engine starts up, the patient experiences vibration, a normal flight stressor. The patient might require more sedation with the increased sensory input.

stem: The nurse arrives on scene after request of an ambulance. The patient is laying on the stretcher and there are no obvious signs of trauma. The EMS provider states the patient has shortness of breath, hemoptysis and the following vital signs: BP: 130/87 mmHg HR: 126 beats/min RR: 35 breaths/min SpO2: 88% on 4L nasal cannula The nurse suspects the patient has:
rationale: This patient is likely suffering from a pulmonary embolism. The classic vital signs include tachycardia, hypoxia, and tachypnea. There are no obvious signs of trauma and a hemothorax without trauma and pneumothorax without trauma is unlikely to be a hemothorax without trauma. Pulmonary hypertension is unlikely based on the presentation.

stem: A patient who was struck by a motor vehicle and sustained bilateral femur fractures is receiving mechanical ventilation. The high-pressure alarms sound and the patient appears restless. Ventilator settings and vital signs are as follows: Mode: volume assist control Rate 16 breaths/min Tidal volume: 560 mL PEEP: 5 cm H2O FiO2: 100% BP 177/121 cm Hg HR 114 beats/min RR 26 breaths/min The transport nurse should recognize that the BEST option to improve ventilator compliance and vital signs is to:
rationale: Administering fentanyl (Sublimaze) would be appropriate for this patient as the patient demonstrates all the classic physiological signs of pain, such as hypertension, tachycardia, restlessness and tachypnea. Adequate sedation and analgesia are paramount for any mechanically ventilated patient.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight crew is requested to complete a patient transport. The nurse recognizes that the two most common factors in fatal helicopter emergency medical accidents are:
rationale: A study by the National Transportation Safety Board revealed that the most frequent factors in HEMS accidents were also pilot error and nighttime operations. This has been validated by subsequent studies. While the other factors are likely contributors, they have not been verified as the most common factors.

stem: The flight nurse is called to transport a patient who has received alteplase (Alteplase) for an occlusion of the right middle cerebral artery. Upon making contact with the patient, the flight nurse recognizes the patient is unresponsive with unequal pupils, which is a change from their baseline stroke assessment. After the airway is managed, the NEXT priority intervention is:
options: Immobilize the head and prepare for transport
rationale: An unresponsive patient with unequal pupils should alert the flight nurse to the potential of new intracranial hemorrhage post Alteplase administration. The patient should have a repeat head CT to assess for hemorrhage, as well as shift. Sedating the patient with ketamine could cause an inaccurate neuro exam. Repeat blood glucose may be necessary but is not a priority intervention over CT. Immobilizing the head and preparing for transport are not appropriate interventions for an unresponsive patient.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The nurse is flying in a helicopter when they enter clouds unexpectedly. The pilot asks the nurse to tune the radio to the emergency frequency. The nurse should tune the radio to:
rationale: The universal emergency frequency is 121.5 MHz. The emergency location transmitter (ELT) operates on 406 MHz. The squawk code for emergency is 7700, however this is not a speaking frequency. 243 MHz is no longer utilized as an emergency frequency.

stem: The flight crew is transporting a patient with a femur fracture via interfacility transfer. The flight nurse has received report from the nurse that the patient was treated with two doses of IV narcotics and one dose IV benzodiazepines, which are documented appropriately on the MAR. The patient is alert and oriented, reporting pain at 10 out 10 and is adamant that no pain medications have been administered. The flight nurse should treat the assessed pain level and:
options: Assess the patient for a history of substance abuse
Confirm to the nurse that long bone fractures are painful and distracting which contributed to missing the administration
Confront the nurse for ineffective pain control
Report the flight crew's concerns of possible diversion to the hospital supervisor
rationale: Per the Joint Commission, 'Appropriate response for staff can be summarized as "see something, say something." At the institutional level, appropriate responses include: establishing a just culture in which reporting drug diversion is encouraged, assessing harm to patients, consulting with public health officials when tampering with injectable medication is suspected, and prompt reporting to enforcement agencies.'

stem: A patient has sustained significant facial trauma from impact with the steering wheel during a collision. Assessment at the scene reveals significant soft-tissue swelling, periorbital, and facial asymmetry with malocclusion. On gentle palpation, the patient's face is mobile and there is increased bleeding from the lacerations. Based on these findings, the nurse suspects:
rationale: The nurse should suspect a Le Fort III craniofacial disjunction. Complete separation of the cranial attachments from the facial bones which causes the face to be free floating when assessed. Le Fort III: pyramidal fracture involving the mid face. It involves a triangular segment of the mid-portion of the face and nasal bones. It does not cause free floating of the face. Mandible fracture is a fracture of the jaw bone. It can be unilateral or bilateral. It does not cause free floating of the face. Maxilla fracture is a fracture of the upper jaw bones, as well as part of the nasal bone and eye socket. It is very similar to a Le Fort II fracture. However, a Le Fort III is bilateral whereas a maxilla fracture is unilateral.

stem: The transport team is called to an outlying hospital for transport of a patient that is 31 weeks pregnant. The sending RN reports the patient is G3P2 and experienced a 'gush' of fluids 24 hours prior to arriving at the hospital. The vitals are as follows: BP: 126/82 mmHg HR: 105 beats/min RR: 20 breaths/min SPO2: 95% (room air) The nurse knows the PRIORITY intervention is:
options: Rapid transport to a maternal center
rationale: The patient has had a PROM (Premature Rupture of Membranes) and the fetus is at risk for neonatal sepsis. There is no need at this point for magnesium. Sterile vaginal exam is contraindicated in the presence of PROM. While rapid transport is indicated, antibiotics should not be delayed.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A patient in a motor vehicle crash has a seatbelt bruise across their upper abdomen. The patient has hypotension and tachycardia after receiving 2L of crystalloid IV fluid. Which of the following should the transport nurse administer FIRST?
rationale: The transport nurse should first administer packed red blood cells. Restoring intravascular volume by carrying capacity is the highest priority.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Multiple flight crew members have expressed concerns over the care abilities of a specific flight nurse and have requested removal from clinical duties. Due to staffing issues, the medical director and leadership elect to allow the flight nurse to continue to staff the clinical crew. If a patient were to receive an injury while under the care of the flight nurse, the air medical provider could be in breach of which of the following professional issues?
rationale: Vicarious liability is an employer's obligation to ensure an employee performs duties safely and competently. Product liability involves the safe use of a manufactured product, collective liability involves when multiple manufacturers collaborate in the production of a faulty product, and alternative liability involves when manufacturers have faulty production in two separate phases of production.

stem: The flight nurse arrives at the scene of a motor vehicle accident. The patient that should be treated FIRST is a:
options: 32-year-old with an obvious deformity to the knee and lower leg
21-year-old with a chest wound who is breathing at 5 breaths/min
1-year-old who has an open head injury and is not breathing
54-year-old who is walking towards the nurse complaining of shortness of breath
rationale: The 21-year-old has decreased respirations, making them the priority patient. The 32-year-old with the injured leg would be the second priority. The 1-year-old would be triaged as 'black', last priority, as the patient is unlikely to survive. The 54-year-old would be triaged as 'green' because they are a walking wounded.

stem: The flight team is transporting an adult patient who presents with altered mental status, diaphoresis, nausea, and vomiting after working in the yard all day. The patient has a patent airway and stable vital signs. An electrocardiogram shows a wide complex rhythm. What is the PRIORITY intervention for this patient?
rationale: This patient is hyperkalemic secondary to acute renal failure. While administering a fluid bolus may benefit this patient, calcium chloride administration will provide the most benefit. Calcium stabilizes the resting membrane potential, thereby stabilizing cardiac tissue and preventing fatal dysrhythmias. Cooling measures may be indicated for elevated body temperature, but will not treat hyperkalemia. Amiodarone will not provide any benefit for a wide complex rhythm resulting from hyperkalemia. There is no indication that the patient requires hypertonic fluid administration.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: While enroute back to base, the pilot declares an in-flight emergency. The flight nurse's PRIORITY action is:
options: Contact air traffic control and declare an emergency
Relayed information about the emergency to relay to dispatch
rationale: The primary role of the transport team during an in-flight emergency is to serve as a resource to the pilot. The first action is to maintain a sterile cockpit so the pilot may aviate, navigate and finally communicate with the med crew. Once the aircraft is under control, the flight team may request a land zone contact dispatch, set the transponder or make emergency radio calls per individual program's policies.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight team was dispatched for a patient who suffered a hard tackle while playing football. On assessment the nurse notes the patient is unable to move their arms or legs and their skin is flushed and warm. Vital signs are: Heart rate: 53 beats/min Blood pressure: 80/47 mmHg Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min SpO2: 94% (room air) Which type of shock is the patient most likely experiencing?
rationale: Neurogenic shock causes the loss of sympathetic tone from a high thoracic or cervical spinal cord injury. This results in vasodilation (pooling of blood in the venous system), decreased systemic vascular resistance, and decreased blood pressure without compensatory tachycardia.

stem: The flight nurse arrives at the scene of a patient who was found after falling the day before. EMS has applied a splint to the obvious left femur fracture. The patient reports that his pain is worse after the splint and his toes are now pale. The nurse's NEXT action should be to:
rationale: The patient may have compartment syndrome. The 5 P's of compartment syndrome are pain out of proportion to the injury, paresthesia, pulselessness, paralysis, and pallor. If caused by a splint or device, it should be loosened. Pain medication can be given after the splint is loosened. If not treated, compartment syndrome could result in the loss of the limb.

stem: The flight nurse responds to an outlying facility to transport a patient with a femur fracture to a larger hospital. The patient is alert and oriented and states they do not want to go due to uncertainty about the cost of transport. The sending nurse insists they must go by helicopter since the physician ordered the transport. The flight nurse should:
options: Assure them insurance will cover the transport
rationale: A patient with decision-making capacity can always refuse transport. The nurse should explain the risks and benefits of refusing transport and obtain a refusal form if the patient wishes to decline. Another option is to contact the patient advocate at your flight program, if available, to provide information on costs and billing so the patient can make an informed decision about their care.

stem: The flight nurse is called to the scene of a scuba diving incident where CPR is in progress. A fellow scuba diver reports the patient rapidly ascended to the surface from where he was briefly alert before going into cardiac arrest. The flight nurse suspects the arrest was likely caused by
rationale: During rapid ascent from depth, expanding gas may form a bubble that travels to the brain causing paralysis, focal seizures, disorientation and unconsciousness. The definitive treatment is a hyperbaric chamber. CPR should be performed along with other interventions including ACLS procedures. The nurse should also inform the pilot to fly at the lowest safe altitude to avoid aggravating the result.

stem: The nurse is monitoring a patient who was recently intubated in-flight for respiratory failure. After descending to the landing pad, the nurse notices the Exhaled Tidal Volume decreased from 450ml to 100ml. The nurse's NEXT action should be to
options: Disconnect the patient from the ventilator and use the bag-mask device
Take a laryngoscope to check ETT placement
rationale: With a descent in altitude, air filled spaces such as the ETT balloon might change in volume. If the cuff is no longer the proper size, a large leak can occur causing decreased exhaled tidal volumes. This is explained by Boyle's law. The ventilator is giving you valuable feedback, using the BMV is not the correct first troubleshooting step. The same is true for reseating the ventilation. While placement might be the cause, the cuff pressure is the more likely culprit.

stem: A patient at a remote construction site has sustained a severe laceration from a broken window. Coworkers have applied direct pressure and an appropriately positioned tourniquet. Despite these measures, active bleeding continues at the wound and the patient appears pale, cool, and clammy. What is the NEXT appropriate action?
options: Loosen the first tourniquet and move it more proximally
rationale: This patient is in hemorrhagic shock. Controlling bleeding is the immediate life-saving measure. Additional tourniquets can be applied to the extremity until the bleeding stops.

stem: The flight nurse is called to transport a patient diagnosed with an open book pelvic fracture. The patient has received multiple blood products and vital signs are stable upon arrival. Upon loading the patient into the helicopter, the nurse notes a significant decrease in blood pressure. The MOST likely cause is:
options: Dislodging of tamponade in the retroperitoneal space
rationale: Retroperitoneal injuries can form hematomas that can eventually tamponade with adequate resuscitation. The movement of a patient can dislodge these hematomas and massive hemorrhage will occur. The acute change in vital signs related to movement in a patient with open book pelvic injury should prompt the flight nurse to suspect bleeding and additional resuscitation should occur. Acute pain would likely cause an increase in blood pressure. Vasovagal response would likely be related to the massive blood loss. Inadequate pain management will likely lead to an increase in blood pressure.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: During a high-altitude environment, a transport nurse notes a decrease in the patient's oxygen saturation. Which of the following gas laws is most likely responsible?
rationale: Dalton's Law is most likely responsible. Dalton's Law refers to the partial pressure of oxygen. At altitude increases, the partial pressure decreases meaning less oxygen is available. Graham's Law refers to the principle that lower molecular weight molecules diffuse at a faster rate than higher molecular weight molecules. This law does not have a direct impact on patients' care or equipment management in the air medical environment. Charles' Law refers to changes in volume as it relates to temperature. Higher temperatures will cause gases to expand. Boyle's Law refers to the change in volume as it relates to altitude. Higher altitudes will cause gas to expand.

stem: The flight team is transporting a patient who is mechanically ventilated. The patient is on assist control ventilation, with a respiratory rate of 16 breaths/min and a tidal volume of 350 mL. The patient is breathing spontaneously. This patient is MOST at risk for:
rationale: A patient who is breathing spontaneously on assist control is able to trigger a breath prior to the ventilator delivering a set breath above the volume set on the ventilator. If the patient hasn't fully exhaled the previous breath, they are at risk for breath stacking. This can lead to increased peak airway pressures and decreased ventilation. Hyperventilation is a long-term concern associated with breath stacking if the breath stacking is not addressed. Atelectasis is not a complication of breath stacking.

stem: The flight team was called to the scene of a music festival to transport a patient who was assaulted. The patient was found with an altered level of consciousness, palpable skull deformities, and a single stab wound to the right upper back. The flight team performed drug-assisted intubation and covered the stab wound with a three-sided dressing. Vital signs are stable. During transport, the patient suddenly develops an elevated Peak Inspiratory Pressure of 45 cm H2O. Current vital signs: BP 72/46 mmHg HR 130 beats/min RR 16 breaths/min SpO2: 79% (100% FiO2) ETCO2: 26 mmHg Glasgow Coma Scale 3 The PRIORITY intervention is to:
rationale: Patients with penetrating trauma to the torso are at risk for open pneumothorax. Positive pressure ventilation can convert a pneumothorax into a tension pneumothorax. This patient's suddenly increased Peak Inspiratory Pressure, tachycardia, hypotension, and hypoxia suggest possible tension pneumothorax. Removing the three-sided dressing and performing needle decompression will temporarily relieve the tension pneumothorax; tube thoracostomy is the definitive treatment, followed by applying an occlusive dressing to the wound.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight nurse responds to a scene call for a patient who is unconscious and has life threatening injuries. Consent for treatment of this patient is:
rationale: Implied consent is operational in emergency situations. Most consent states allow for treatment of life-threatening emergencies if the patient is unable to consent because it is the reasonable thing to do. The other terms do not apply.

stem: The 7 Ps of rapid sequence induction include which of the following?
rationale: The 7Ps of RSI are: preparation, preoxygenation, pretreatment, paralysis, protection and positioning, placement with proof and postintubation management. The other choices are not applicable.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight nurse is called to transport a patient diagnosed with intraparenchymal hemorrhage. The patient initially presents with a Glasgow Coma Score (GCS) of 15 and stable vital signs. During flight, the patient's GCS decreases to 10 and the team determines the patient needs an advanced airway. The flight nurse knows what about preparing for advanced airway management in the patient with intracranial hemorrhage?
options: Cervical spine immobilization is only required if injury is present
Smaller doses of induction medications are indicated
rationale: BLS airways in this patient population may lead to coughing and gagging which can increase intracranial pressures. They should be avoided until the patient is adequately sedated. Paralytics should not be confused with sedation. Smaller doses of medications are not indicated. Cervical spine immobilization should be maintained in this patient population regardless of injury, until definitive spine movement has been ruled out.

stem: The flight team is called to a scene for an adult with multiple gunshot wounds to the chest and abdomen. The patient is intubated. The transport nurse notes an absence of breath sounds in the left lung fields. The chest tube to the right chest has a positive air leak and is functioning without issue, however, there is no drainage or air leak present in the left chest tube system. The ED resident urged the patient is transported now and let the accepting trauma team address this upon arrival. The flight nurse should:
options: Request to speak to the attending physician
rationale: The patient with a non-functioning chest tube needs this resolved prior to departure and ascending to altitude; further blood accumulation in the chest can potentiate hemodynamic collapse. While the ED resident might be responsible for the patient, there is a chain of command within the ED and getting an attending physician involved, in this instance, should help in getting another set of hands to assist. If the attending physician declined, calling the Trauma Center's accepting MD may be warranted, and/or medical directors. Transporting this patient without a functioning chest tube, on a long duration transport, could exacerbate the situation further. Boyle's law should be considered. A needle decompression will only help remove the air, not the blood accumulating in the chest.

stem: A patient with an unstable pelvis was found 6 hours after falling down a ravine and is being evacuated from the scene via helicopter. They were placed on oxygen at 6 L/min via nasal cannula and have the following vital signs: BP 80/45 mm Hg HR 98 beats/min RR 32 breaths/min O2 Sat 100% ETCO2 22 mm Hg The flight nurse recognizes this patient is MOST likely suffering from:
rationale: The low ETCO2 value coupled with vital signs consistent with shock are indicative of a patient in anaerobic metabolism. Neurogenic shock is incorrect because in neurogenic shock bradycardia would be expected. Permissive hypotension is incorrect because there is no indication of an intentional choice to not resuscitate the patient. Pulmonary contusion is incorrect because with a pulmonary contusion it is unlikely the oxygen saturation would be 100% on 6 L.

stem: A 34-week pregnant patient arrived at a community emergency room with a severe headache. She was found to have proteinuria, acute kidney injury, and no signs of active labor. Her blood pressure was monitored and she was initiated on a magnesium sulfate infusion. The flight nurse understands that the purpose of the magnesium sulfate infusion is to:
rationale: This patient is showing signs and symptoms of preeclampsia, which increases her risk of seizures. Although magnesium can be used as a tocolytic to prevent preterm labor, in this case, it is being used to prevent seizures because it raises the seizure threshold.

stem: The flight nurse is called to transport a 3-year-old child who became unresponsive after playing with an older sibling. According to the mother, a barking sound was heard from the room where the child was playing with small building blocks, and she suspects the child may have swallowed one. Upon arrival, a ground crew is manually ventilating the child. While preparing for rapid sequence induction, the flight crew should have which item available?
rationale: The flight nurse should suspect foreign body aspiration in this child. Magill forceps should be available during the RSI procedure to remove the foreign object once it is visualized, before placing the endotracheal tube. A supraglottic airway may not be used until the foreign body has been removed to avoid further migration into the airway. Humidified oxygen and succinic oxygenation are used to treat croup, but are not appropriate in this situation as the foreign body takes priority.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A flight crew member who recently had a dental filling placed suffers severe dental pain to the tooth during the ascent phase of flight. This condition is the result of which gas law?
rationale: Boyle's law states that the volume of gas is inversely related to atmospheric pressure. As altitude increases, air expands and small portions of air grow larger. In crew members, this may occur following recent dental surgery. Graham's Law states that the diffusion rate of a gas is inversely proportional to the square root of the mass of its particles. Charles's Law states that gases expand when temperature increases. Gay-Lussac's Law states that pressure of a given mass is directly related to the absolute temperature.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Following an explosion at a manufacturing plant, first responders are preparing a landing zone for the inbound transport team. The landing zone should be located:
options: Upwind and uphill from the scene
Downwind and downhill from the scene
rationale: Being upwind prevents the wind from bringing chemicals towards the team; being uphill is necessary because some chemicals accumulate in low-lying areas. Being upwind and uphill prevents rotor wash from spreading chemicals around the area. The landing zone will need to be away from patient care areas to prevent rotor wash from spreading chemicals.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight crew responds to the bedside of a patient with a glioblastoma. The nurse recognizes that the patient's increased intracranial pressure is a result of:
rationale: The Monroe-Kellie doctrine states that cranial vault is composed of brain, blood, and CSF. When a condition changes volume in one area it decreases the others and results in an increase in intracranial pressure (ICP). Boyle's law describes the relationship of volume and pressure when temperature is constant. Cushing's syndrome describes the physiologic effects of increased ICP. Cerebral autoregulation is the mechanism by which the brain maintains perfusion by varying pressure.

stem: The transport team is transporting a 2-year-old child who has a suspected bacterial infection. The child is responsive to painful stimuli, and has mottled skin. The patient's vital signs are as follows: BP 60/30 mm Hg HR 180 beats/min RR 10 breaths/min T 94.1°F (34.5°C) Which of the following interventions would be the highest priority
options: Initiate a normal saline bolus of 20 mL/kg
rationale: Initiating a normal saline bolus of 20 mL/kg is correct because this patient is in uncompensated septic shock. Restoring an adequate circulating volume is the highest priority. Administering adenosine and performing synchronized cardioversion is incorrect because the heart rate is due to profound shock, not SVT. Administering a broad spectrum of antibiotics is incorrect because resuscitation to correct the fluid deficit comes before antibiotic administration.

stem: The nurse lands on scene of a farm accident. The patient has suffered an amputation of the left arm. The bleeding is controlled and the vitals are stable. When preparing the extremity for transport, it should be:
options: Wrapped in dry gauze, in a plastic bag, on dry ice
Wrapped in a saline-filled plastic bag, on ice
Wrapped in saline-soaked gauze, in a plastic bag, on ice
Wrapped in dry gauze, on saline-soaked gauze, placed below
rationale: It is recommended to wrap the extremity in wet gauze or a clean sheet, then place in a bag on ice. It is not recommended to use dry ice.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The dispatchers have not been able to contact the transport vehicle for greater than 20 minutes, and the crew has not arrived at the destination. The dispatcher should activate the
rationale: A post accident incident plan is a program policy that outlines the responsibilities of the communications specialist and program administration of a transport program during and immediately after an accident or incident. A disaster plan is developed for a group of people at a facility that details what needs to be done, how, when, and by whom before and after the time an anticipated disastrous event is expected to occur. There is no such thing as an unscheduled event plan. Incident command plan is a management concept designed to clarify command relationships from different responding agencies during a disaster or mass casualty event requiring a multi-jurisdictional response.

stem: The flight crew is called to transport a 6-year-old child following a submersion injury. The child is awake, alert, and breathing spontaneously but was reported to have been underwater for 2 minutes and required CPR when EMS arrived. Which intervention should the flight nurse perform before transport?
options: Administer 20 mL/kg of isotonic fluid bolus
rationale: Although the child's respiratory condition requires close monitoring over the next few days, their alertness and wakefulness are reassuring signs despite the drowning incident. A cervical collar should be placed in case a fall or diving injury caused the drowning incident. If vital signs are stable, a fluid bolus can be withheld unless the patient shows signs of decompensation.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: For informed consent, in addition to explaining the risks and benefits to the patient, the transport nurse should also include
options: Signs and symptoms leading to the need for the procedure
The length of time of the procedure
rationale: Informed consent includes all of the following essential components: description of procedure to be performed; available alternatives to procedure; risks and benefits of procedure and alternatives; patient understanding of procedure, alternatives, and associated risks/benefits of all; practitioner or physician presence during consent administration.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: While preparing for transfer, the flight nurse fails to properly secure the patient to the transport sled. During a hard landing, the patient sustains injuries. The nurse could be found guilty of:
rationale: Malpractice is committed when all of the required elements are present: presence of duty, breach of duty, foreseeability, causation, injury and damages. Foreseeability and causation are elements that must be present for malpractice to occur, but are not the actual violation. Malfeasance is a type of breach of duty caused by an act of commission. The question describes nonfeasance, or an act of omission.

stem: The flight crew is transporting a patient with a diagnosis of MI to a specialty resource hospital. When comparing the patient's presenting ECG to the flight crew's ECG, the crew interprets the presence of Q-waves greater than 1 mm wide with a deflection half the height and depth of the entire complex. The flight crew recognizes the ECG changes as
rationale: Presence of a Q-wave has been associated with transmural AMI; however, studies now demonstrate that both Q-wave (STEMI) and non-Q-wave (non-STEMI) infarcts can be transmural or subendocardial. In general, Q-wave infarctions are associated with a larger region of myocardial necrosis, higher enzyme levels, fresh coronary thrombosis, frequent vomiting, congestive HF, conduction defects, dysrhythmias, and loss collateral circulation.

stem: A tree trimmer, who was electrocuted after making contact with an overhead power line, has full-thickness burns on the right hand and foot and a painful, tight right forearm. During transport, the patient is screaming and writhing in pain despite several doses of analgesics. The cardiac monitor shows sinus rhythm with frequent multifocal PVCs. The flight nurse PRIORITIZES which intervention:
options: Elevate right arm to the level of the heart
Apply a cold compress to the right arm
Emergent escharotomy of the right forearm
rationale: Electrical burns pose a risk of compartment syndrome. This patient reports extreme pain, even though their contact burns are full-thickness and treatable. The abnormal appearance of their forearm needs careful monitoring. The arm should be elevated to heart level to enhance blood flow. While electrical burns also carry a risk of cardiac instability and arrhythmias, the PVCs currently do not require intervention. If conservative management does not improve the condition of the right arm, a fasciotomy should be considered as the next step. An escharotomy is only necessary in cases of full-thickness circumferential burns. Applying a cold compress is contraindicated for this injury.

stem: The flight nurse responds to a patient from Florida who arrived at a mountain resort area two days prior. The patient complains of severe headache, fatigue and dyspnea at rest. Vital signs are: HR 115 beats/min RR 28 breaths/min with dry cough BP 142/88 mmHg SpO2 68% (2L nasal cannula) What disorder is this patient likely suffering from and what (apart from oxygen administration) is the most beneficial treatment for this patient?
rationale: HAPS can occur at altitudes between 8000-10000 feet and is usually associated with rate of ascent to altitude and typically occurs 2 days after arrival at altitude. Presentation is dyspnea at rest, a dry cough, rales, tachypnea and tachycardia. The treatment is oxygen and descent to increase the partial pressure of oxygen. If there is a change in the level of consciousness or development of seizures then HACE must be ruled out.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: When evaluating a landing zone during a scene response, the HIGHEST priority for the flight crew is:
rationale: Safety is the flight crew's chief priority. When evaluating a potential landing zone, identifying obstructions is one of the steps to ensure the scene is safe. Ensuring ground personnel contact, communicating with dispatch, and monitoring wind direction are each important but not the highest priority in evaluating the landing zone from the air.

stem: A flight team is dispatched to transport a patient with circumferential full-thickness burns to the chest. The patient is intubated and placed on a ventilator. The flight nurse notes the peak inspiratory pressure to be 45 cm H2O. Which of the following is the BEST intervention for this finding?
options: Switch the mode to pressure control
rationale: Due to the loss of elasticity of the tissue following a burn, it may become difficult to ventilate a patient. Performing escharotomies will enable effective ventilation. Although suctioning the patient may help with oxygenation, it will not change the lung compliance. As this is a burn patient with bilateral breath sounds, a tube thoracostomy is not indicated at this point. Changing the mode to pressure control will not change the decreased lung compliance caused by the circumferential burns to the chest.

stem: The flight crew is transporting an infant who was delivered at 34 weeks and presents with increasing lethargy and cool skin. The PRIORITY laboratory assessment should be
rationale: Preterm newborns are prone to hypoglycemia due to impaired glycogen storage. This child may not have enough glycogen stored to maintain a normal blood glucose level, as most glycogen storage occurs during the third trimester. Lactate, sodium, and PaCO2 are not the priority labs used to identify the immediate life threat.

stem: The transport team has been called to a Labor and Delivery unit to transport a 32-week pregnant patient with gestational diabetes, Group B strep, and influenza to a tertiary facility. While receiving report, which of the following findings would lead the transport nurse to suspect that labor is imminent?
rationale: Cervical dilation exceeding 4 cm indicates impending labor, regardless of previous pregnancies. A -3 fetal station indicates the presenting part is situated 3 cm above the ischial spines and has not yet entered the true pelvis. A cervical effacement of 20% is considered mild and usually does not indicate significant progression toward labor. Normal moderate variability in fetal heart rate is expected and does not imply that labor is imminent. The flight nurse can expect preterm infants to deliver at less than 10 cm dilation.

stem: The flight nurse has responded to a patient who was trapped in a burning house. On initial assessment, singed facial hair, erythema of the oropharynx and carbonaceous sputum is noted. Which pathophysiologic change should be INITIALLY anticipated when managing the ventilation of this patient?
rationale: Noxious chemicals from combustion adhere to the surface of the smoke particles and cause direct damage to the airway epithelium, especially in the smaller airways and terminal bronchi which impairs oxygen exchange. Inhalation injuries do not usually cause spontaneous pneumothorax although aggressive positive pressure ventilation could cause a pneumothorax. Pneumonia would be a more delayed concern. Inhalation injuries cause hypoxia versus hypercarbia.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: After arriving on scene of a rollover accident on the highway involving a tractor-trailer marked with hazardous materials placards, the Incident Commander directs the flight team to:
options: Don Level D personal protective equipment and begin treatment in the warm zone
Initiate universal personal protective equipment (PPE) and begin treatment in the cold zone
Stage in the landing zone, pending identification of the hazardous materials
Don Level A personal protective equipment and begin triage in the warm zone
rationale: Patients in the cold zone have already been decontaminated, thus universal PPE is appropriate. The warm zone is where decontamination takes place; as such, the flight suit, which is a form of Level D PPE, is not adequate protection. Level A PPE is the highest level protection with a fully encapsulated suit and self-contained air supply; these are reserved for the highest trained hazardous materials technicians. Identifying the hazardous materials is important, but treatment can begin as soon as decontamination takes place.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight crew is flying over water when the pilot informs the team that there is an in-flight emergency, and his intention is to 'ditch the helicopter.' The flight nurse knows it is important to:
options: Release the seatbelt prior to impact
Kick feet during to clear doors
Allow the helicopter to completely invert before trying to exit
Exit the helicopter immediately after impact
rationale: During a water landing in a helicopter, it is important to wait for the craft to fully invert and all but fill with water prior to attempting egress. Trying to exit prior to this can lead to the helicopter capsizing on top of you or getting obstructed by the blades and debris. Kicking is to be avoided during egress as you could inadvertently become tangled or injure a coworker. You should not release your seat belt prior to impact to avoid being ejected or thrown about the cabin.

stem: The nurse is transporting a 78-year-old patient from a community ED to a tertiary hospital for altered mental status. Prolonged QT segments are observed on the monitor. The head CT was negative and the toxicology screen was also negative. Medication history includes Trandate (Labetalol), Lasix (Furosemide), and Aspirin. The blood chemistries are included on the transfer form: Glucose: 149 mg/dL Calcium: 9.1 mg/dL PCO2: 29 mmHg Chloride: 105 mEq/L Sodium: 136 mEq/L Potassium: 2.0 mEq/L Blood urea nitrogen (BUN): 30 mg/dL Creatinine: 1.5 mg/dL The correct action for the nurse is to:
rationale: The elderly patient usually is on many medications and may take them incorrectly. This patient took too much Lasix causing a dangerous drop in Potassium. The patient can have fatal arrhythmias due to QT prolongation and is not the priority. Calcium is within the normal range, therefore calcium gluconate is not the priority.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: What term describes stress that is considered normal, encountered in daily living, and may enhance function, heighten awareness, or provide new perspectives?
rationale: Eustress is a type of stress that is nonthreatening and generally makes humans perform better. Distress involves noxious stimuli. Somatization and cognitive are not types of stress.

stem: During resuscitation of a polytrauma patient, the flight nurse knows what lab value is indicative of impaired perfusion and increased mortality rate.
rationale: Elevated lactate (greater than 4 mmol/L) is indicative of increased anaerobic metabolism and indicative of oxygen deficit within the tissues, which shows a decrease in perfusion. Potassium and sodium are not indicative of poor perfusion. Base excess of -2 is within a normal range.

stem: While transporting a pregnant patient in preterm labor, the transport nurse notes fetal bradycardia and finds a prolapsed umbilical cord. Initial intervention should include
options: Manual displacement of the presenting part
Attempted replacement of the umbilical cord
Placing the patient in the supine position
rationale: Elevation of the presenting part with a hand in the vagina is done to prevent further cord compression. Positioning in Trendelenburg or the knee-chest position can also be attempted. Supine maternal positioning will not help and may additionally cause maternal hypotension due to supine hypotension syndrome. Attempted replacement of the umbilical cord would be dangerous. Although the cord may spontaneously retract, the cord should never be manually replaced because severe compression may occur. Tocolysis is indicated to slow contractions and reduce the pressure on the cord.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: When administering medications to the geriatric patient population, the flight nurse understands that:
options: The patient has less fat reserves, so larger doses should be administered
The patient is less sensitive to medications, so larger doses should be administered
The patient's liver function is lower, so lower doses should be administered
The patient has not increased kidney function, so smaller doses should be administered
rationale: The older adult generally has lower function of end organs and increased fat reserves. Medications should be given carefully as they will not be metabolized as quickly as in the younger adult population.

stem: On arrival to the scene of a high-speed MVC, the flight crew receives a patient lying in the ambulance that is pregnant at 32-weeks gestation. The patient is awake and alert, complaining of sharp suprapubic pain. The patient reports a maternal history of G2, P1 and received prenatal care. The patient has a previous history of a c-section. Assessment reveals asymmetrical uterus and rebound tenderness. Based on this assessment, the nurse is concerned for which of the following conditions?
rationale: Classic signs of uterine rupture are an asymmetric uterus, suprapubic pain, and palpable fetal parts. Ovarian lacerations typically present with signs and symptoms of retroperitoneal bleeding. Classic signs and symptoms of abruptio placenta are dark red vaginal bleeding, a board-like uterus, and a rising fundal height. Preterm labor does not fit this clinical picture.

stem: The flight nurse is called to a community hospital for a neonate in distress. The newborn is cyanotic and has an oxygen saturation of 60% on room air which worsened with high-flow oxygen. Prior to transport, the nurse should request administration of:
rationale: This infant is likely suffering from a transposition of the great arteries. The ductus arteriosus needs to be kept open until surgical correction can be performed. Prostaglandins should be administered cautiously, as it can cause apnea. These patients usually require intubation. The other medications would not be indicated in this case.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The transport team is on scene of a chemical release at a manufacturing plant. The team notices numerous patients walking towards them vomiting and exhibiting urinary and fecal incontinence. The team should:
options: Identify the source of the contaminant
Don level A personal protective equipment
Properly administer Atropine to the patients
rationale: The patients are exhibiting signs and symptoms of exposure to organophosphate pesticides or nerve agent chemicals, thus they need to be decontaminated prior to treatment. Atropine is used as an antidote to organophosphates and nerve agents, the transport team cannot begin treating patients until they are decontaminated. Identifying the source of the contaminant is necessary, but is a lower priority than protecting the transport team's safety.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Following an emergency landing, a critical incident stress debriefing is held. Two months later, a flight nurse who was involved in the incident seems uninterested in work, startles easily, and complains of frequent flashbacks and nightmares. Which of the following should the manager do?
options: Arrange for the nurse to receive individual counseling
Evaluate the nurse for substance abuse
Remove the nurse from duty immediately
Advise this is a normal stress response for the nurse
rationale: This is not normal stress because it is interfering with daily living. Removing the nurse from duty and having the nurse tested for substance abuse would be punitive in nature and should be avoided. Arranging for individual counseling for the nurse to work through the issue with a professional counselor fits with current practices of CISM.

stem: During transport, a patient suffers cardiopulmonary arrest. The flight nurse proceeds with intubation to secure an airway. Which method of assessment for determining proper ETT placement is BEST in this situation?
options: Absence of air movement in stomach
rationale: Proper ETCO2 waveform is the best method of assessment.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight nurse enters an ambulance and finds several paramedics and police officers attempting to restrain a combative patient who is actively bleeding from a self-inflicted wrist laceration. The patient is resisting care and is stating 'let me die! I want to die!' Which type of consent allows the flight nurse to treat the patient?
rationale: A patient who is suicidal and in police custody or under a physician's emergency commitment can not refuse life-saving treatments. This self-inflicted wound may be fatal without immediate treatment. Patients that are suicidal, homicidal, delusional, or experiencing other psychiatric emergencies may not be able to think rationally and the flight nurse can legally treat the patient, even if resisting care, when acting in good faith.

stem: A patient with atrial fibrillation complains of a sudden onset of pain and numbness in the right forearm. The arm is cool to the touch and has a thready radial pulse. The transport nurse should suspect which of the following?
rationale: The nurse should suspect arterial thrombosis. Arterial thrombosis is a common complication of atrial fibrillation. An embolus which mobilizes into the upper extremities would manifest itself with these findings. Venous insufficiency is most commonly associated with the lower extremities and is a chronic condition. Classic findings include venous ulcers, skin discoloration, and enlarged or varicose veins. Compartment syndrome is an acute finding, generally associated with trauma. Long bone fractures are present in as much as 20% of all patients presenting with compartment syndrome. Other causes such as drug abuse, anticoagulant use, and other chronic medical conditions have also been associated with compartment syndrome. Myocardial ischemia is commonly manifested with pain radiating to the left arm and neck, and does not usually cause vascular changes to the upper extremities.

stem: A transport nurse observes blood in the catheter connection tubing of a patient receiving intra-aortic balloon (IABP) therapy. The nurse should FIRST
options: Disconnect and manually inflate the balloon
Clamp the connection tubing to the balloon catheter
rationale: The presence of blood in the catheter connection tubing is the hallmark of balloon rupture. It requires immediate cessation of pumping and removal of the IABP. Continued use of a ruptured balloon can lead to helium gas embolization, a potentially fatal event. The balloon is most likely ruptured. Although intact balloons that are not actively pumping should be intermittently, manually inflated according to the manufacturer's directions to prevent clot formation, manual inflation of a ruptured balloon will inject gas into the aorta through the defect in the balloon. Flushing and re-zeroing of the arterial line are appropriate only if the balloon is intact. These measures will not clear blood in the catheter tubing. Although the desired action in the presence of balloon rupture is to prevent gas pumping into the balloon, the correct course of action is to cease pumping, not merely clamp the connection tubing.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: A flight team is transporting a patient by fixed-wing aircraft at a cruising altitude of 40,000 feet. The flight nurse observes a fog-like mist within the interior of the cabin. The nurse's FIRST action should be to
options: Ensure that all passengers and equipment are secured
Check outlets and medical equipment for smoke
rationale: The nurse should first begin breathing supplemental oxygen. Mist or 'fog' within the aircraft's cabin at altitude most likely indicates decompression. The nurse should first ensure that he or she is protected from hypoxia as a result of exposure to reduced barometric pressure. Administering oxygen to the patient is a secondary action. Time of useful consciousness at 40,000 feet is 15 seconds or less. If the nurse is disabled by hypoxia, then further helpful actions would not be possible.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: In a helicopter safety and landing zone (LZ) presentation to a local EMS service, which of the following is the MOST important instruction for EMS personnel?
options: Approach only from the loading side of the helicopter
Utilize ambulance lights to highlight the LZ
Ensure LZ is at least 200' x 200'
Proceed toward the aircraft only when directed by the pilot
rationale: The scene call environment is one of the most potentially hazardous aspects of air medical operations. Safety is of paramount importance and situational awareness must be maintained when working around an aircraft. Ground personnel should only be allowed to approach the aircraft when directed to do so by the pilot or a flight crew member. The pilot in command of the aircraft must be able to see personnel approaching. Mitigating the hazard from air medical operations on ground personnel is the most important instruction. The LZ can be a minimum of 75' x 75' during the day and 125' x 125' at night. While some situations may require lighting the landing zone, the MOST important instruction for EMS personnel should be to approach only when directed.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: Which of the following cranial nerves is responsible for pupillary response in a closed head injury patient?
rationale: Oculomotor is correct. Oculomotor (CN III) is responsible for pupillary response. Optic Nerve (CN II) is visual acuity, visual fields, reading printed material, and seeing light. Trigeminal is responsible for corneal reflex and facial sensation. Abducens (CN VI) is responsible for extraocular eye movement.

stem: The flight team is called to transport a 6-month-old child diagnosed with febrile seizures. Upon arrival, hospital staff states the child has lost IV access and two additional attempts have been made to initiate access, which have been unsuccessful. Vital signs: BP 68/40 mmHg HR 178 beats/min RR 36 breaths/min T(rectal) 103°F (39.4C) O2 Sat 99% (room air) The NEXT appropriate stop is:
rationale: This child is presenting with shock, elevated HR and low BP. It is pertinent to obtain access on this child to initiate fluid resuscitation. Intraosseous access can be obtained quickly, and fluid resuscitation can be started without further delay. The other three options would delay fluid administration and therefore could be harmful to the patient outcome.

stem: The flight nurse is called to an accident scene where a patient has been trapped under a fallen tree for several hours and has a crushed leg. IV access has been established. As the patient is extracted, the nurse should prepare to administer
rationale: Crush injuries can cause a large release of potassium. Calcium gluconate is used to neutralize the effects of hyperkalemia. Fentanyl can be given after or before for pain control but is not the priority. Normal saline can be given for volume replacement but is again not the priority. Magnesium chloride would not be a correct choice for this patient.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: During a rotor scene call for a 4-year-old patient, the patient's mother requests to ride along with the patient to the hospital. Which of the following statements by the mother would MOST concern the flight crew?
options: 'I would like to speak to the pilot about safety of the flight'
'If I can't hold my child, they won't go with you'
rationale: The decision to transport a family member is based on multiple factors, and while all of the choices may weigh in the decision, safety of the entire team must be the primary factor. Family members should be instructed that safety regulations require all passengers to wear approved restraint devices (Commercial Flight Rule 135.128).

stem: The flight crew is called to transport a 6-year-old child who was hit in the chest with a baseball. The patient has had a chest x-ray that shows no fractures to the sternum or ribs. The flight nurse is MOST concerned about which of the following vital signs?
rationale: Children have flexible ribs that are not fully formed, and therefore may not fracture with blunt injuries. Blunt cardiac injuries cannot be ruled out in a pediatric patient with known chest trauma. Cardiac arrhythmias (ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation) are likely in the patient with blunt chest trauma. Because the pediatric patient cannot compensate with stroke volume changes, a decreased blood pressure is a concerning sign following thoracic trauma.

stem: A school-age patient presents after a house fire with 3rd degree circumferential burns of the neck and chest. Physical exam reveals the extent of the burn is 60% total body surface area. The patient also presents with stridor and drooling. Vital signs are: BP: 90/45 mmHg HR: 133 beats/min RR: 28 breaths/min O2 Sat: 100% (room air) GCS: 14 The flight nurse understands that the PRIORITY intervention is:
rationale: The patient is demonstrating signs and symptoms of smoke inhalation and younger patients are at greater risk for airway obstruction. Oxygen saturation may be inaccurate due to carbon monoxide exposure from an enclosed area. The priority intervention is to protect the airway and initiate early intubation. IV fluids, dressings, and wound debridement are all indicated for burn resuscitation, but they are not the priority.

stem: The nurse is transporting a patient who has suffered an epidural hematoma. What is the BEST indication of a change in intracranial pressure?
rationale: When assessing the patient with a neurologic injury, level of consciousness is the best indication of changes in ICP.

stem: The flight team responds to a facility for a high risk OB transport of a mother with gestational diabetes. The baby has just been delivered and is born limp. A heel stick reveals a blood glucose of 25 mg/dL and an umbilical line has been placed by the delivery team. Appropriate treatment is to administer:
rationale: Critically ill hypoglycemic neonates require IV glucose infusions of 4-6 mg/kg/min of D10W. The other doses and concentrations are not appropriate for this age group.

stem: A patient is being transported by air for direct admission to a medical intensive care unit after ingesting a large quantity of ethylene glycol. After verifying the position of the patient's endotracheal tube and that he is breathing spontaneously, the nurse notes that the ETCO2 is 15 mm Hg. The nurse should anticipate the patient will need emergency
rationale: The nurse should anticipate hemodialysis. Ingestion of more than 1 mL/kg of ethylene glycol can often be fatal. Metabolism of ethylene glycol deposit in the kidney, causing renal failure.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight crew is called to the scene of a collision where heavy front end damage to the vehicle has caused the vehicle to catch fire. A back seat passenger reports they were able to self extricate after 10 minutes and reports no physical burns. Upon assessment, the nurse notes the patient has soot to their face, and a dry voice. The MOST appropriate next action is:
options: Monitor for airway compromise from inhalation burns
Triage the patient to the basic life support (BLS) ambulance
Transport the patient to the closest facility
rationale: Soot on the face and raspy voice are indicative of heavy inhalation burns. This patient should be closely monitored for airway compromise and transported to the nearest burns center. IV fluids important, but not the most important intervention. A BLS ambulance is not recommended due to the potential for airway compromise.

category: General Principles of Flight Transport Nursing Practice
stem: The flight nurse is preparing to transport a patient who was injured and has significant swelling and paresthesia to the left upper extremity. Which order by the sending facility should be questioned?
options: Elevate the extremity above the heart
rationale: An extremity with compartment syndrome has reduced blood flow and elevation could further decrease circulation. Immobilization and splinting are appropriate, but caution should be taken not to apply excessive pressure that might impair blood flow. Regular reassessment is necessary to detect any worsening of the condition. The patient may also need analgesics for pain management.

stem: A patient was a restrained driver in a side-impact motor vehicle accident. On assessment, the patient is awake and verbalizing difficulty breathing. The patient appears pale and diaphoretic, tachypneic, and has decreased breath sounds over left lung field. Bony crepitus is palpated over the left chest. Vital signs are as follows: BP 78/56 mm Hg HR 132 beats/min O2 sat 84% The flight nurse should suspect the patient may be experiencing:
rationale: Blunt trauma can cause massive hemothorax as a result of rapid accumulation of >1500 ml of blood in the chest cavity. Rapid accumulation of blood results in severe hypotension and hypoxia. The information from the assessment does not provide mechanism of injury for a cardiac tamponade or assessment findings, such as muffled heart tones. Cardiac tamponade patients are not usually hypoxic. The patient could have a pneumothorax but simple pneumothorax patients are not usually pale and hypotensive. The assessment does not demonstrate a floating segment or inward movement of the chest wall during inspiration and outward movement during expiration.

stem: Which of the following is the MOST appropriate intervention to prevent and protect the patient with and to reduce the risk for pulmonary barotrauma from mechanical ventilation?
options: Attempt to lower the plateau pressure
Increase the fraction of inspired oxygen
rationale: Attempting to lower the plateau pressure is the most appropriate intervention. Maintaining a plateau pressure less than or equal to 35 cm H2O is desired. The other interventions are not the most appropriate interventions. Initiating permissive hypocapnia strategies would mean increasing the tidal volume or the respiratory rate. Both actions may further the barotrauma. Decreasing expiration time is not appropriate, because decreasing the inspiration time will increase the peak inspiratory pressure. Increasing the fraction of inspired oxygen will have no effect on barotrauma.
