rationale: Mental status (LOC) decline appraises cerebral perfusion. Capillary refill and skin color and temperature evaluate circulatory status. Cyanosis is a late sign of hypoxia.

stem: Which indication for airway management is a distinctive factor of consideration for the transport team?
options: Protection of the airway in patients with an increased risk of aspiration.
Pulmonary toileting to remove blood and secretions.
Chemical restraint to manage combative behavior.
Oxygenation for the patient in respiratory distress or shock.
rationale: Chemical restraint or sedation may be required to ensure safety of all in ground, and especially air transport. Deep sedation may cause the patient's inability to protect the airway or respiratory depression.

stem: Interpret the following arterial blood gas (ABG): pH: 7.25, PaCO2: 40, HCO3: 15.
rationale: Metabolic acidosis is seen in ketoacidosis, salicylate, methanol, and ethylene glycol ingestion, renal failure, diarrhea, and lactic acidosis from shock. Normal pH: 7.35-7.45 (7.36-7.44), PaCO2: 35-45 (34-44), HCO3: 22-26.

stem: Interpret the following arterial blood gas (ABG): pH: 7.33, PaCO2: 32, HCO3: 18.
rationale: Partially compensated metabolic acidosis is seen as patients increase respiration to blow off CO2 to compensate for acidosis, but the pH is not back to normal. Normal pH: 7.35-7.45 (7.36-7.44), PaCO2: 35-45 (34-44), HCO3: 22-26.

stem: Interpret the following arterial blood gas (ABG): pH: 7.36, PaCO2: 48, HCO3: 30.
rationale: Compensated respiratory acidosis is seen when the pH is back to normal. The primary acid-base disorder is respiratory acidosis since the pH is < 7.40 and the PaCO2 is > 45. HCO3 is increased, creating the compensation. Normal pH: 7.35-7.45 (7.36-7.44), PaCO2: 35-45 (34-44), HCO3: 22-26.

stem: The Mallampati score describes the ability to see structures in the oropharynx to predict intubation difficulty. Which score is given when the soft palate, uvula, fauces, and tonsillar pillars are visible?
rationale: Mallampati I is when the soft palate, uvula, fauces, and tonsillar pillars are visible. Intubation is typically not difficult in class I and II airways.

stem: You are performing a needle cricothyrotomy on a patient with severe facial trauma and inability to ventilate via noninvasive techniques. You have stabilized the patient's head, identified the cricothyroid membrane, prepared the skin and are stabilizing with your nondominant hand. You have a 12-guage over-the-needle intravenous catheter in your dominant hand. At what angle do you insert the catheter into the cricothyroid membrane?
rationale: The needle and catheter are inserted at a 45-degree angle caudally (toward the feet). The needle is removed, and the catheter is advanced caudally. The hub of the catheter can be connected to a 3.0-mm endotracheal tube connector and can be ventilated with a bag-mask device. This is a temporary measure until a more secure airway can be attained.

stem: Which induction agent is preferred in head trauma since it has little to no effect on mean arterial pressure (MAP) and decreases cerebral blood flow and intracranial pressure (ICP)?
rationale: Etomidate is preferred in head trauma patients since it has little to no effect on mean arterial pressure and decreases intracranial pressure. Drawbacks of etomidate include it provides no analgesia, it causes pain during injection and possible adrenal suppression.

stem: Which induction agent is unique because it provides amnesia and analgesia?
rationale: Ketamine is a dissociative anesthetic providing amnesia and analgesia. Premedication with atropine may be needed to reduce airway secretions. Benzodiazepines can reduce the incidence of emergence reactions.

stem: Succinylcholine (Anectine) should be avoided in patients with which electrolyte imbalance?
rationale: Succinylcholine should be avoided in the presence of hyperkalemia since it may cause cardiac dysrhythmias.

stem: You are transferring a patient who was intubated for respiratory failure using etomidate and succinylcholine. While receiving report from the nurse, you note that the patient's heart rate increases to 120 bpm, muscle rigidity is present, and ETCO2 increases to 60 mm Hg. What medication should be administered?
rationale: Malignant hyperthermia is a rare life-threatening condition that can be caused by succinylcholine. Signs and symptoms include muscle rigidity/spasm, increased ETCO2, tachycardia, and hyperthermia. Dantrolene sodium (Dantrium) is the treatment, along with manual cooling.

stem: What is the amount of blood in the ventricles at end-diastole?
rationale: Preload is the volume of blood in the ventricles at end-diastole. Starlings law describes how increasing preload increases the ventricular stretch and subsequent contraction. Right preload is measured by central venous pressure (CVP) and left preload is measured by pulmonary artery obstructive pressure (PAOP). Normal CVP ranges from 2-6 mm Hg in adults and normal PAOP range is 8-12 mm Hg.

stem: What is the resistance the ventricles must overcome to eject blood into the pulmonary and systemic circulation?
rationale: Afterload is the resistance the ventricles must overcome to eject blood into the pulmonary and systemic circulation. Normal systemic vascular resistance (SVR) is 800-1200 dynes/s/cm-5. The formula is (MAP-CVP)/CO x 80. Afterload is decreased in distributive shock (septic, neurogenic, and anaphylactic), and increased in hypovolemic, cardiogenic, and obstructive shock.

stem: Systemic vascular resistance (afterload) is decreased in which type of shock?
rationale: Afterload (resistance) is decreased in distributive shock (neurogenic, septic, and anaphylactic) due to vasodilation, and high in all other forms of shock due to vasoconstriction. Normal SVR is 800-1200 dynes/s/cm-5.

stem: What hemodynamic parameters are seen in early sepsis?
options: Increased CO and SV, decreased SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
Decreased CO, SV, SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
Decreased CO, SV, CVP, and PAOP, increased SVR.
Decreased CO and SV, increased SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
rationale: In the early (hyperdynamic phase) of sepsis the patient is febrile, skin is flushed, and pulses are bounding. Early hemodynamic indicators of sepsis are an increase in cardiac output and stroke volume, as well as an increase in oxygen consumption and SvO2, with a decrease in SVR (distributive shock), CVP, PA, and PAOP. Treat with 30 mL/kg (2.1 L for 70-kg), antibiotics, and Norepinephrine initially.

stem: What hemodynamic parameters are seen in hypovolemic shock?
options: Increased CO and SV, decreased SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
Decreased CO, SV, SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
Decreased CO, SV, CVP, and PAOP, increased SVR.
Decreased CO and SV, increased SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
rationale: In hypovolemic shock, there is a decrease in cardiac output, stroke volume, CVP, and PAOP, with an increased SVR. The patient has lost volume, so right and left preload is low, decreasing cardiac output and stroke volume. Systemic vascular resistance (SVR) is increased due to catecholamine release to compensate for shock state. Initial treatment is focused on appropriate volume replacement.

stem: What hemodynamic parameters are seen in cardiogenic shock?
options: Increased CO and SV, decreased SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
Decreased CO, SV, SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
Decreased CO, SV, CVP, and PAOP, increased SVR.
Decreased CO and SV, increased SVR, CVP, and PAOP.
rationale: In cardiogenic shock, there is a decrease in cardiac output and stroke volume, with an increased SVR, CVP, and PAOP. The patient has left ventricular pump failure, so right and left preload is high due to inability to pump, decreasing cardiac output and stroke volume. Systemic vascular resistance (SVR) is increased. Initial treatment is focused on decreasing preload with diuretics and decreasing preload and afterload with nitroglycerin.

stem: You are transporting a 78-year-old two-day post anterior MI patient who complains of chest pain. Respirations are 28 and labored, heart rate of 120 and a blood pressure of 80/44. Skin is cool and clammy, and crackles are audible bilaterally. Hemodynamics reveal the following: PAOP of 30 mmHg, SVR of 1600 dynes/sec/cm, CI of 1.4 L/min/m2. The nurse should expect the patient's immediate treatment to include which of the following?
options: Positive inotropic agents and fibrinolytic therapy
rationale: The patient is in cardiogenic shock, so needs vasodilators to decrease afterload (resistance) and positive inotropes to increase contractility and blood pressure. Beta-blockers are negative inotropes and would decrease contractility. Fluids would worsen the already increased left ventricular preload indicated by an SVR of 1600 dynes/sec/cm.

stem: You are preparing to transport a 25-year-old male patient who has been intubated in the referring facility's emergency department. The patient has an 8.0 endotracheal tube (ETT) in place, secured at 24 cm at the teeth. You notice his heart rate has increased to 140 bpm, SpO2 decreases to 79%, and blood pressure decreases to 89/45. Lung sounds are absent on the left side, clear on the right. What is your initial action?
options: Request a STAT x-ray to determine tube placement.
Pull the endotracheal tube (ETT) back 1-2cm to relieve right mainstem intubation.
Emergent needle decompression to left chest.
Request ED attending at bedside to place chest tube prior to transport.
rationale: The patient is showing signs of a tension pneumothorax on the left side which is an emergency requiring emergent needle decompression of the plural space with a 14-gauge, 2-inch needle. The change is sudden, so a right mainstem intubation is unlikely. The endotracheal tube is also placed at the proper depth, 3 times the size of the tube.

stem: What is the recommended site when inserting a chest tube?
rationale: The chest tube is inserted into the 4th-5th intercostal space, anterior axillary line (AAL) or mid-axillary line (MAL). A needle decompression may be performed in this location, or more commonly, in the 2nd ICS, midclavicular line. Insert the needle over the top of the rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle.

stem: You are transporting a 39-year-old female patient weighing 80 kg diagnosed with status asthmaticus on a ventilator. Current ventilator settings are as follows: Vt 480, rate 18, FiO2 0.8, PEEP 5, I:E 1:2. The PIP is reading at 52, EtCO2 56, and SpO2 95%. What ventilator changes should be made to manage this patient?
rationale: In patients with reactive airway disease, change the I:E ratio to 1:4 or 1:5 (normally 1:2 or 1:3) to allow longer expiratory time and prevent \"breath-stacking\" or auto-PEEP.

stem: You are assessing an intubated and mechanically ventilated patient. Ventilator settings are as follows: Vt 480, rate 18, FiO2 0.6, PEEP 5, I:E ratio 1:2. ABG reveals pH 7.39, PaCO2 41, HCO3 23, PaO2 52. What is the proper management of this patient?
rationale: The PaO2 is < 60, indicating hypoxemia. Increasing FiO2 and PEEP can increase oxygen levels. Increasing Vt and rate will help to decrease CO2.

stem: You are assessing a patient in respiratory failure and determine that the patient requires intubation. Respiratory failure is defined as:
options: PaO2 < 60 mm Hg and PaCO2 \> 50 mm Hg
PaO2 < 80 mm Hg and PaCO2 \> 60 mm Hg
PaO2 < 60 mm Hg and PaCO2 \> 30 mm Hg
PaO2 < 90 mm Hg and PaCO2 \> 50 mm Hg
rationale: Acute respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation is defined as PaO2 < 60 mm Hg and PaCO2 > 50 mm Hg.

stem: A left shift in the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve can be caused by:
rationale: Hypothermia. Left shift increases the affinity of oxygen to hemoglobin, making it easier to pick up but harder to release. Causes of Left shift include Low temperature (hypothermia), Low 2-3, DPG, Low PaCO2, aLkalosis. Right shift is caused by hypeRthermia, increased 2,3-DPG levels, high PaCO2, acidosis (think Raised=Right, Low=Left).

stem: You are transporting a 70-kg patient with ARDS who is intubated and ventilated. Current ventilator settings are as follows: Vt 900mL, rate 14, FiO2 0.8, PEEP 5. ABG's reveal pH 7.34, PaO2 52, PaCO2 38, and HCO3 24. You are experiencing high-pressure alarms on the ventilator. All the following are appropriate actions, EXCEPT:
rationale: Decreasing rate. Ventilator management for ARDS involves using the \"injury approach\" which protects lungs from further harm. This is done by decreasing tidal volumes, starting at about 4mL/kg, and increasing rates to maintain minute ventilation, increasing PEEP to increase and maintain alveolar recruitment and oxygenation, and increasing FiO2 as necessary.

stem: The diagnosis of acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) would most likely reveal which chest x-ray findings?
options: Hyperinflation, narrow and elongated heart shadow, increased A/P diameter of the chest.
rationale: Chest x-ray findings in ARDS include widespread \"ground-glass\" appearing infiltrates, diffuse infiltrates. ARDS is characterized by hypoxemia refractory to oxygen therapy.

stem: You achieve ROSC after performing CPR on a patient. The patient remains unresponsive and apneic, HR is 96 and BP is 90/46. Hypothermia, sedation, and neuromuscular blockade agents are initiated. The transport nurse will place the patient on which mode of ventilation?
rationale: Assist-Control ventilation delivers a preset tidal volume for every breath and is best for paralyzed patients. SIMV allows for intermittent patient breaths, pressure support requires that the patient be breathing on their own.

stem: A 70-kg patient is being ventilated in SIMV mode, with a Vt 500 ml, FiO2 of 40%, rate of 18 breaths/minute, PEEP of 5cm H2O. ABG's results: pH 7.50, PaCO2 25, PaO2 84, HCO3 24. Which order for ventilator changes do you anticipate?
rationale: Decrease the rate to keep the patient from blowing off too much CO2, resulting in respiratory alkalosis. Decreasing the rate is the initial intervention to correct the acid-base imbalance. Breath rate is determined by PaCO2.

stem: A 70-kg patient admitted with pulmonary contusion after an MVC has developed ARDS. She is being ventilated in assist control (AC) mode, with a Vt of 400 ml, FiO2 of 60%, rate of 12 breaths/minute, PEEP of 10cm H2O. ABG's show pH 7.22, PaCO2 65, PaO2 60, HCO3 24. What is the MOST appropriate intervention?
options: Increase the tidal volume to 8.5 ml/kg
rationale: The ABG shows respiratory acidosis, so the patient needs to increase respiration to blow off CO2. Increasing tidal volume would help eliminate CO2 also but is NOT appropriate in ARDS for lung protection.

stem: What inhaled medication may be used to decrease pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) in pulmonary hypertension?
rationale: Inhaled nitric oxide (iNO) used for pulmonary vasodilation, decreasing pulmonary vascular resistance.

stem: What inhaled medication may be used for post extubation stridor?
rationale: Helium-oxygen mixture (Heliox) reduces the resistance to airflow, which may reduce the need for reintubation.

stem: What is a normal shock index (SI)?
rationale: Shock index (SI) is the heart rate divided by the systolic blood pressure. The normal range is 0.5 to 0.7, and it is inversely proportional to cardiac output and mean arterial pressure. Shock index is an early indicator of shock, and values over 1.0 are associated with worsened outcomes.

stem: A patient with a blood pressure of 260/120 (MAP 167) is in hypertensive crisis and being treated with IV nitroprusside (Nipride). Over the next 60-120 minutes, the nurse expects to titrate the Nipride infusion to a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of:
rationale: Reduce the MAP by only 25% over the first 1-2 hours. The initial MAP is 167 \[(120+120) + 260\] = 500 divided by 3 = 167. 75% of 167 is 125 mm Hg. Management of Increased ICP (Reference) I -- Intubate C -- Calm/Coma P -- Place drain/Paralyze H -- Hypertonic saline E -- Elevate head A -- Adequate BP D -- Diuretic (Mannitol)

stem: What classification of medications may mask the early signs of hypovolemic shock?
rationale: Beta-blockers may mask the early signs of shock by preventing an increase in heart rate.

stem: During a 45-minute flight to a level one trauma center, what is crucial in the management of the patient with a traumatic pneumocephalus?
options: Instruct the pilot that the patient has a condition that requires flying at a low altitude.
Burr hole craniotomy to temporarily lower intracranial pressure.
rationale: Instruct the pilot that the patient has a condition that requires flying at a low altitude. The pilot will determine the safe altitude to fly the patient with a pneumocephalus (air within the cranium). Hyperosmolar therapy such as mannitol or hypertonic saline reduce plasma volume. A craniectomy must be performed by a neurosurgeon. Burr holes may be performed for a bleed by a trained, non-surgeon provider if the patient is acutely deteriorating or neurological intervention is several hours away.

stem: Excluding brain herniation, why is hyperventilation to achieve a PaCO2 < 35 mm Hg contraindicated in patients with traumatic brain injury?
options: It decreases venous return to the brain.
It causes cerebral vasoconstriction, which decreases cerebral blood flow.
It increases seizure risk, causing secondary brain injury.
rationale: Hyperventilation causes cerebral vasoconstriction, which decreases cerebral blood flow. The patient's CO2 should be maintained between 35 to 45 mm Hg throughout the transport to avoid cerebral ischemia.

stem: A patient was beaten with a baseball bat and intubated on arrival to the trauma bay due to head trauma. His initial ICP was 20 mm Hg, but while awaiting transport his intracranial pressure (ICP) has climbed to 40 mm Hg. He currently has a SpO2 of 97%, PaCO2 of 40, and MAP of 92 mm Hg. What emergent intervention does the nurse anticipate?
options: Administration of Norepinephrine (Levophed) to increase CPP.
Administration of Labetalol to manage blood pressure.
Sustained hyperventilation to facilitate CO2 exhalation.
rationale: Administration of Mannitol IV. Hyperosmolar therapy, such as mannitol and hypertonic saline are used to treat increasing ICP. An IV bolus of mannitol 1 gm/kg should be given over 5 minutes through a filter. Since Mannitol is an osmotic diuretic, electrolytes such as sodium, and serum osmolality should be monitored closely. Mannitol should not be given to patients with existing hypotension.

stem: A 42-year-old female is transported to the ED after a fall from 30 feet with massive facial trauma with obvious fractures to both upper extremities. Head CT shows a subdural hematoma with a midline shift of 3mm. Her GCS is 3 and she remains hypotensive despite blood transfusions. The trauma nurse anticipates an order for which medication?
rationale: Hypertonic saline is just as effective, and potentially more effective, at lowering ICP than mannitol. Since Mannitol is an osmotic diuretic, it is contraindicated in patients with existing hypotension from hypovolemia. Steroids (Solu-cortef) are not indicated in head injury.

stem: A teardrop-shaped pupil with leakage of vitreous humor is most associated with which eye injury?
rationale: Globe rupture is characterized by an irregular pupil, periorbital ecchymosis, decreased visual acuity and extraocular movements (EOMs), deep eye pain, and nausea.

stem: A 1-gram IV bolus of Tranexamic acid (TXA) was administered to a patient with a traumatic brain injury. What follow-up IV dose should the nurse prepare to administer prior to transport?
rationale: TXA is administered in two doses with the first dose of 1 gram given over 10 minutes and the second dose is 1 gram over 8 hours.

stem: Why is calcium replacement necessary in massive transfusion?
options: Rapid infusers hemolyze cells, resulting in hypocalcemia.
Most traumas are due to alcohol abuse, and patients with alcoholism are typically hypocalcemic.
The preservative citrate in banked blood chelates calcium, leaving it inactive.
rationale: Citrate binds with calcium, leaving it inactive. Calcium is essential for clotting, so the patient continues to bleed, worsening hemorrhagic shock. Guide replacement on the patient's serum ionized calcium levels.

stem: You are at the scene of a 49-year-old male patient who was ejected from his motorcycle. The left side of his chest has been decompressed with a needle and the patient has been intubated. His SpO2 continues to decline and you assess subcutaneous emphysema (SQ air) on the left side of the patient's chest and neck. What is the next appropriate intervention?
options: Advance the ET tube into the right mainstem, below the level of the injury.
rationale: Advance the ET tube to provide one-lung ventilation. Tracheobronchial injury occurs most often from blunt trauma. S/S can include failure of lung to re-expand after needle thoracostomy or chest tube placement, tension pneumothorax, subcutaneous emphysema. Treatment is to immediately intubate with the ETT placed below the level of the injury for one-lung ventilation.

stem: An unrestrained roll-over MVC patient presents with abdominal tenderness and distension. VS: HR=140, RR=40, BP=74/50. Based on the nurse's knowledge of balanced resuscitation, what intervention is anticipated?
options: 2 liters of warmed crystalloids and 2 units warmed packed RBC's.
4 units warmed PRBC's, 4 units FFP, equal volume of platelets.
1 unit of warmed PRBC's and 2 units FFP.
rationale: Start with 4 units of blood, FFP, platelets. This patient is in acute hypovolemic shock from blood loss and requires massive transfusion at 1:1:1 of PRBC's, FFP, and platelets. Electrolyte abnormalities after massive resuscitation include hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, and potassium irregularities.

stem: A patient with a head injury is initially unresponsive, then had a lucid period before losing consciousness again. Which of the following cerebral arteries is most involved in an epidural bleed (EDH)?
rationale: The middle meningeal artery is the most injured in epidural hemorrhage, along with a linear skull fracture. It is an arterial bleed between the skull and the dura. It appears as a lentiform, biconvex shape on head CT (visual may be on exam). A ventricular drain may be placed to monitor ICP and drain CSF.

stem: A 20-year-old male is being transported to a level 1 trauma center with an ice pick penetrating his neck. He can speak with a hoarse voice. What is the best manner to transport this patient?
options: Remove the ice pick and hold direct pressure during transport.
Use a rigid cervical collar to stabilize the ice pick.
Perform a cricothyrotomy to maintain a patent airway.
rationale: Transport the patient with the object stabilized with bandages, not a cervical collar.

stem: You arrive on the scene of a patient who was hit in the abdomen with a baseball bat. The patient complains of difficulty breathing and severe left shoulder pain. Vital signs are B/P 80/49, HR 133, RR 38, SpO2 89%. Upon assessment, you notice a scaphoid abdomen and hear peristaltic sounds on the left side of the chest. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Diaphragm and spleen. Bowel sounds in the chest cavity are indicative of a diaphragmatic injury which is more likely with blunt trauma. Spleen injuries often occur with left sided diaphragmatic trauma. Left sided diaphragm rupture is more common because the liver often absorbs the impact of the force if injured on the right side.

stem: Which of the following findings would be consistent with the diagnosis of hemothorax?
options: Dullness to percussion, absent breath sounds on the affected side.
Hyperresonance to percussion, absent breath sounds on affected side.
Paradoxical chest wall movement, subcutaneous emphysema.
Dysphagia, bowel sounds audible in thoracic cavity.
rationale: Dullness to percussion, with absent breath sounds on affected side. Hyperresonance is heard in air filled space (pneumothorax), dullness from fluid (hemothorax). Paradoxical chest wall movement is seen in flail chest, bowel sounds in chest in ruptured diaphragm. Immediate blood return of 1500 ml or 200 ml/hour for 4 to 5 hours is an indication for surgical intervention.

stem: Discrepancy between blood pressure values and pulse strength, a loud systolic murmur, and mediastinal widening on a chest radiograph are symptoms of which traumatic injury?
rationale: Aortic disruption should be considered in severe deceleration injury, especially with first and second rib fracture. S/S include severe chest pain, dyspnea, a loud systolic murmur, blood pressure and pulse strength discrepancies with decreased pulses in lower extremities, and mediastinal widening and obscured aortic knob (apical cap) on a chest radiograph (visual may be on exam). Start two large-bore IV's, maintain heart rate < 100 bpm, and SBP < 120 mm Hg.

stem: A patient with low systolic blood pressure (narrowing pulse pressure), jugular venous distention, and muffled heart sounds is most likely experiencing which of the following?
rationale: These three medical signs make up Beck's triad which are associated with cardiac tamponade. Pulsus paradoxus (a fall in systolic blood pressure > 15mm Hg during normal inspiration) may also be observed. Treatment includes IV fluid boluses to keep SBP 90 to 100 mm Hg, emergent pericardiocentesis at the left xiphostal region 15-30 angle toward left shoulder, and rapid transport.

stem: When managing a patient with an adult with electrical injury that presents with blood-tinged urine, you should maintain a urine output of:
rationale: If hemochromogen urine is present after an electrical injury, urine output must be maintained at least 100 mL/hour to prevent acute kidney injury. Sodium bicarbonate may be used for systemic/urine alkalinization.

stem: You are transporting a patient with the following vital signs: HR=78, BP=150/90. The ICP monitor is reading 15 mm Hg. What is the patient's cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP)?
rationale: 95 mm Hg is the cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP). Cerebral Perfusion Pressure (CPP) is calculated by subtracting the ICP from the MAP. Normal CPP is 70-90 mmHg, normal ICP is 3-15 mmHg (numbers vary per text). In the incidence of increased ICP, MAP should be maintained at 70-90 to keep CPP 60-70 mm Hg. In this scenario, the MAP is 110 mmHg, calculated with the formula MAP = \[(DBP x 2) + SBP\] / 3. \[(90x2) + 150\]/3 = 110. 110-15 = 95 mm Hg.

stem: A patient with a stab wound to the right chest is restless and tachypneic. Assessment reveals absent breath sounds on the right side and tracheal deviation to the left side. What is the priority management?
options: Intubation with fiberoptics to allow one-lung ventilation.
Pericardiocentesis with an 18-gauge, 7 to 9 cm needle, introduced at the left xiphocostal angle, aiming for the right shoulder.
Emergent decompression of the pleural space with a 14 or 16-gauge, 7 cm needle in the right 2nd intercostal space, mid-clavicular line, over the third rib.
rationale: A tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening situation requiring emergent decompression of the pleural space, followed by tube thoracostomy.

stem: Which of the following is an incomplete spinal cord syndrome in which you would see a greater loss of function in the arms than the legs?
rationale: Central cord injury presents with a greater loss in arms than in legs. Anterior cord syndrome has the worst prognosis and results in a loss of pain and temperature sensation below the level of the injury with spared proprioception and vibration. Brown-Sequard lesion results in an ipsilateral motor and vibratory sense loss and contralateral pain and temperature loss below the level of the injury.

stem: Predictable injuries that can occur when a motorcycle rider "lays down" his bike often include the following, EXCEPT:
rationale: Pelvic fractures occur more often when the rider is thrown off the motorcycle. Injuries seen in "lay down" trauma when the extremity is crushed between the motorcycle and the ground include abrading the skin on the same side, shoulder, and tib/fib fractures.

stem: When decontaminating lime and other dry chemicals you should:
options: Neutralize with a special agent prior to irrigation.
Wrap in a dressing and not irrigate.
Irrigate immediately with water or saline.
rationale: Dry chemicals should be brushed off before irrigation. Chemical burns differ from thermal burns in that the burning process continues until the agent is inactivated by being neutralized or diluted with water.

stem: You are assessing a patient on the scene of a motor vehicle accident in which the patient was ejected from the vehicle. The patient is warm and flushed and states that he cannot move his arms or legs. The right forearm was splinted by EMS prior to arrival, and they reported obvious deformity. Vital signs are as follows: BP 79/42, HR 45, RR 18, SpO2 95% on room air. What kind of shock is this patient most likely experiencing?
rationale: Neurogenic shock is a form of distributive shock. Although trauma patients are at risk of hypovolemic shock due to bleeding, this patient does not show evidence of extensive blood loss. The decreased heart rate with likely spinal cord injury above T6 is a hallmark sign of neurogenic shock. Neurogenic shock is caused by loss of sympathetic stimulation leaving unopposed parasympathetic stimulation. Treat with fluid resuscitation, norepinephrine, and dopamine to increase preload and afterload.

stem: Which situation would warrant performing an escharotomy?
options: Circumferential burns to the chest and legs when transport time will be greater than 30 minutes.
Circumferential burns to the extremities or digits that have adequate circulation.
Circumferential burns to the chest that decrease chest wall compliance.
Circumferential burns present on any pediatric patient.
rationale: Circumferential burns to the chest that decrease chest wall compliance create respiratory insufficiency and hypoxia. Perform an escharotomy to allow chest to expand, then reassess ventilation.

stem: Which symptom is a classic early sign of increased compartmental pressure?
options: Increased pain with movement of the affected extremity
rationale: The earliest sign of compartment syndrome is unrelenting burning pain that is out of proportion to the injury, even with passive movement. The other options may be later signs of compartment syndrome, but do not always occur. Keep the extremity in neutral position and prepare for a fasciotomy if the pressure rises > 30 mm Hg.

stem: A man was sawing a board and cut off his finger. What is the appropriate care for the amputated part?
options: Drop the finger into a bottle of sterile saline.
Wrap it in saline-moistened gauze and place in a plastic bag on top of ice.
Place it in a bag with ice directly covering the part.
Clean it vigorously and tightly wrap it in place attached to stump.
rationale: Gently rinse amputated parts in sterile saline and wrap in saline-moistened gauze. Place it in a bag on ice to chill but do not place it directly on ice or allow it to freeze. Do not use dry ice to cool.

stem: A patient has a pyramidal maxillary bone fracture involving the mid-face. Assessment findings include facial edema, nasal fracture, epistaxis, and malocclusion. This fracture is known as a:
rationale: Le Fort II maxillary fracture is pyramidal with concurrent nasal fracture and epistaxis. A transverse detachment of the maxilla above the teeth with concurrent lip laceration is commonly seen with a Le Fort I fracture. Massive facial swelling is seen more with Le Fort III fractures. Zygoma fractures cause a flattening of the cheekbone. Increase the head of the bed during transport due to the risk of blood aspiration.

stem: In which of the following facial fractures is airway assessment most vital because the tongue can lose control and occlude the airway?
rationale: Mandibular fracture is characterized by malocclusion, drooling, and paresthesia of the lower jaw. The tongue is attached to the mandible, so keep the patient in high fowlers position and prepare to secure airway.

stem: What is the treatment for a retrobulbar hematoma?
options: Ice packs to the orbit for 48 hours.
rationale: Emergency decompression via lateral canthotomy is the treatment for a retrobulbar hematoma. Intraocular pressures are frequently > 40 mm Hg (normal IOP < 20 mm Hg).

stem: You are treating a patient who has been burned with hydrofluoric acid. After thoroughly irrigating the burn, what medication should be applied to the burn?
options: No medication should be applied, wrap the burn in dry sterile dressings.
rationale: When treating hydrofluoric acid burns, calcium gluconate gel should be applied to the burn after irrigating for 30 minutes or until pain relief is achieved.

stem: A 16-year-old male riding a jet ski crashes into a pier. On arrival to the trauma bay his vital signs are HR=136, RR=32, and BP=84/62. The patient has a "frog leg" appearance and scrotal swelling. A pelvic film shows an open book pelvic fracture. What is the nurse's priority intervention for this patient?
options: Check for pelvic stability by rocking the pelvis.
rationale: Apply a pelvic binder. An open book pelvic fracture is caused by anterior-posterior compression. With the symphysis pubis separated the posterior pelvic venous complex and the internal iliac artery can hemorrhage. Internally rotating the lower limbs and stabilizing with a pelvic binder reduces the pelvic volume; binding the pelvis creates a smaller area to collect blood. The initial intervention is to apply a sheet or pelvic binder at the level of the greater trochanters of the femur. After stopping the hemorrhage, the patient should receive balanced resuscitation until taken to the OR.

stem: A 28-year-old male admitted for surgical repair of a mid-shaft femur fracture becomes confused, diaphoretic, anxious, and short of breath. The nurse notices a petechial rash is noted on his chest, and vital signs are HR=132, RR=36, BP=128/74, O2 sat 85%. What does the nurse suspect?
options: Hypovolemia due to blood loss during surgery
rationale: Fat embolism. Embolic marrow can become dislodged during the manipulation of long bone fractures. The marrow moves to the lungs causing an obstruction known as a fat embolism, seen most 12-72 hours after a long bone fracture. S/S: altered mental status (restlessness, agitation), acute respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, and a petechial rash to head, neck, anterior thorax, or axillae.

stem: A 69-year-old female discovered twelve hours after a fall where she fractured her hip has developed rhabdomyolysis. What causes the initial dark red or brown urine in rhabdomyolysis?
options: Myoglobinuria is the result of muscle injury, causing dark urine.
Hydroxocobalamin is administered for treatment, breaking down myoglobin into vitamin B 12.
Deferoxamine is administered for treatment, breaking down myoglobin into iron.
Rhabdomyolysis causes disseminated intravascular coagulation, causing hematuria.
rationale: Rhabdomyolysis is caused by muscle-tissue breakdown causing myoglobin to release resulting in dark brown (cola or tea-colored) urine. CK and potassium elevate. TX: IV fluid resuscitation to maintain a urine output of 100--300 mL/hour. Adding sodium bicarbonate to the IVF to alkalinize the urine may enhance myoglobin excretion.

stem: EMS transports a patient from a housefire with burns to the face, neck, and chest. The patient has stridor and is coughing up carbonaceous sputum. What is the priority intervention?
options: Calculate the total surface burn area for fluid resuscitation.
Send blood to lab for a carboxyhemoglobin to assess for carbon monoxide toxicity.
Assess the patient for the odor of bitter almonds to determine cyanide toxicity.
rationale: Prepare for immediate intubation before the onset of edema and complete obstruction occurs. Take care when securing ETT to burned tissue.

stem: A trauma patient is slightly anxious, with a HR=96, RR=16, and a BP=120/80. Identify the class of hemorrhagic shock.
rationale: Class I since pulse is still < 100, (10-15 % blood loss). Pulse increases (> 100) and pulse pressure narrows at Class II, BP decreases at Class III, anuria at Class IV.

stem: A 100-kg adult presents with extensive partial thickness & full-thickness burns from a housefire, measuring 50% body surface area (BSA) & requires fluid resuscitation. According to American Burn Association (ABA) guidelines (Modified Brooke formula), what amount of fluid should the patient receive in the first 8 hours after injury?
rationale: 5000 mL. ABA guidelines require adults with burns with > 20% BSA (partial and/or full thickness) to receive fluid resuscitation with LR at 2 ml x kg x % BSA burn. Ex: 2 ml x 100 kg = 200 x 50% burn = 10,000/24 hours; ½ = 5,000 in first 8 hours and ½ = 5,000 over next 16 hours. Urinary output of 30-50ml/hour for adult is most important.

stem: A 20-year-old with thermal burns has been fluid resuscitated using the Modified Brooke formula as recommended by the American Burn Association. Which of the following findings is reflective of physiological stabilization?
options: Urine output of 0.5-1 ml kg per hour
rationale: Normal urinary output is 0.5-1.0 ml/kg/hour for adults and 1-1.5 ml/kg/hour in a pediatric patient. Urinary output is the best measurement of normalizing organ perfusion and cardiac output. A respiratory rate of 24 likely indicates an acidotic state related to hypoperfusion. A heart rate of 120 shows continued compensation for low cardiac output and hypoperfusion. Hypotension indicates uncompensated shock.

stem: A female patient arrives at the ED after escaping a house fire. There are no obvious burns, but the patient is confused and complaining of a headache and nausea. VS: HR=76, RR=18, BP=130/78, O2 sat 99% on room air. A non-rebreather mask (NRB) is applied at 15 L/min and a carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) level is drawn. The lab calls to report a COHb level of 32. What is the priority nursing intervention for this patient?
options: Discontinuing the O2 since the oxygen saturation is 98% and the respiratory rate is WNL.
Oxygen should be delivered at 15 L via NRB until the COHb level is less than 10.
The nurse should prepare for intubation due to mechanism of injury.
An IV should be obtained to give IV steroids to reduce swelling.
rationale: Deliver oxygen via 15 L via NRB until COHb normalizes (<10%). The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve shifts to the left in CO toxicity, so hemoglobin holds onto oxygen, yielding pulse oximetry unreliable. All patients exposed to CO should receive high-flow oxygen to expedite the separation of CO from the Hgb molecule. Pregnant patients are more likely to need hyperbaric oxygenation to support fetal circulation.

stem: A 16-year-old patient presents to the emergency department after drain cleaner is splashed in his left eye. The priority intervention is:
options: Copious irrigation with NS or LR.
Contacting his parents for permission to treat.
rationale: Copious irrigation with NS or LR. Alkalis like drain cleaner can cause liquification necrosis. Use NS or LR at body temperature (37 C). Irrigate until measurement of the ocular pH stays at neutral level (7.0-7.6) for 30 minutes.

stem: A patient with a posterior hip dislocation is most likely to suffer from which complication?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
rationale: A dislocated hip may result in avascular necrosis of the femoral head and sciatic nerve damage without timely reduction.

stem: A 21-year-old patient who was previously treated for a clavicle fracture and an anteriorly dislocated shoulder returns to the emergency department the next day with their affected arm hanging limply at their side. The nurse recognizes the patient is experiencing what complication of the dislocation?
rationale: Shoulder and elbow dislocations can often lead to brachial plexus damage which can present up to 48 hours after the injury.

stem: A 67-year-old male complains of shortness of breath and tightness in his chest. VS: HR=108, RR=22, BP=164/94. ECG shows ST segment elevation in V2, V3, and V4. Which coronary artery is likely occluded?
rationale: Leads V1-V4 reflect the left anterior descending artery (LAD). Leads II, III, and aVF reflect the right coronary artery.

stem: ST segment elevation in EKG leads I, aVL, V5, and V6 indicates a STEMI in which area of the heart?
rationale: Leads I, aVL, V5, and V6 look at the lateral wall of the left ventricle. II, III, and aVF look at the inferior wall. V1-V4 look at the anterior wall, V4R looks at the right ventricle.

stem: A patient complaining of epigastric pain has ST segment elevation in ECG leads II, III, and aVF. She is diagnosed with an inferior MI and NTG is administered sublingually. Her blood pressure drops from 120/74 to 90/60 so the provider orders a right-sided ECG to detect right ventricular MI's. Where is the lead placement for V4R?
rationale: The ECG lead for V4R is placed at the 5th intercostal space, right mid-clavicular line. In the inferior leads, a right-sided ECG should be performed.

stem: Which of the following conditions is commonly associated with bradycardias and second degree, type I heart block?
options: Occlusion of the right coronary artery
Occlusion of the left anterior descending artery
rationale: Bradycardia and second-degree type I heart block (Wenckebach) is associated with inferior MI's (RCA occlusion in most) since the right coronary artery supplies the AV node.

stem: A patient with Marfan's syndrome is restless, pale, and complains of a sudden onset of severe ripping chest to scapular pain. VS: HR=98 bpm. The left brachial BP=180/90, the right brachial BP=150/70. High-flow oxygen and two large bore IVs are initiated. Blood products are ordered for transfusion and the transport team is on site. What is the next priority intervention for this patient?
options: Allow the family at the bedside since the mortality is high for aortic dissections.
Obtain a chest x-ray to assess for mediastinal widening and left apical cap.
Administer Labetalol to lower heart rate and blood pressure to reduce the dissecting force.
Infuse nitroprusside (Nipride) to rapidly lower blood pressure.
rationale: Once oxygen and IVs are initiated, the priority is to control the heart rate and blood pressure with beta-blockers like labetalol to pause the progression of the dissecting force. Allow the family to see the patient prior to transport if feasible.

stem: Your patient becomes unresponsive, apneic, and pulseless. You see a ventricular fibrillation rhythm on the cardiac monitor. What is the priority intervention?
options: Insert an intraosseous needle into the humerus and administer epinephrine 1 mg.
rationale: High-quality CPR and early defibrillation are crucial in cardiac arrest when the patient is in a shockable rhythm. Medications and definitive airways are secondary to basic life support. Self-adhesive defib pads increase crew safety. The initial pediatric defibrillation wattage is 2-4 joules/kg.

stem: A patient with a right ventricular MI becomes hypotensive. Which is the initial treatment to increase preload?
rationale: 250 cc isotonic fluid boluses are the initial treatment. Positive inotropes such as Dobutamine are used to increase contractility. Use caution with nitroglycerin (NTG).

stem: You notice a cardiac rhythm on a patient who is apneic and pulseless that shows organized electrical activity with P waves and QRS complexes but no pulse. What is the rhythm and immediate intervention?
options: Sinus bradycardia; prepare for immediate tracheal intubation.
Sinus bradycardia; administer Atropine 0.5 mg IV/IO.
PEA; begin chest compressions, administer epinephrine, and consider the H's and T's.
PEA; start bag-valve-mask ventilations giving one breath every 6 seconds.
rationale: In cardiac arrest from pulseless electrical activity (PEA) the priority is high-quality CPR, administering epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO every 3-5 minutes, and considering the H's and T's to find and treat the cause of the arrest.

stem: You are preparing to administer nitroglycerin to a patient complaining of chest pain. Which patient history would concern the nurse enough to further investigate before giving the medication?
rationale: Nitroglycerin is contraindicated in patients taking phosphodiesterase inhibitors for erectile dysfunction within the last 24-36 hours depending on the specific medication.

stem: A 50-year-old male complains of fever and retrosternal chest pain that worsens with inspiration. Assessment reveals a pericardial friction rub, so the nurse expects to see which ECG changes?
options: Flattened T wave with U wave
Peaked T wave with wide QRS
rationale: Global concave ST segment elevation in ECG leads except V1 and aVR is seen in pericarditis. Treat with oxygen via nasal cannula, NSAIDs, and steroids.

stem: Which heart block is characterized by a progressive prolongation of the PR interval that eventually blocks an impulse?
rationale: Second degree, type 1 (Wenckebach) is characterized by a progressive prolongation of the PR interval that eventually blocks an impulse, dropping a QRS, then the pattern is repeated.

stem: You are caring for a patient post pacemaker insertion. The monitor showed a 100% ventricular paced rhythm initially after insertion, but the rhythm has changed to a sinus bradycardia at 30 bpm without spikes. The nurse suspects which pacemaker malfunction?
rationale: Failure to pace is characterized by no electrical activity or spike from the pacemaker. Failure to capture is a spike but no QRS. Failure to sense is when the pacer does not detect the native cardiac activity and pacing occurs randomly. The transport team should carry additional batteries for transport.

stem: While transporting a patient with an Impella device inserted, the patient goes into cardiac arrest. What is the appropriate action of the flight nurse?
options: Remove the catheter so that the patient can be defibrillated without damaging the machine.
Reduce the flow rate to P-2 and perform CPR.
Increase the flow rate to P-8 and allow the device to circulate blood for the patient.
Purge the system and perform CPR.
rationale: The Impella catheter is attached to a controller with speeds of P-1 to P-9. When CPR is required, reduce the flow rate to P-2. Other Impella tips are to keep it plugged, battery life is 60 minutes. Do not increase the head of the bed > 30 degrees.

stem: Intra-Aortic balloon pumps (IABP) have the following hemodynamic effects?
rationale: IABP increases coronary artery perfusion, which increases myocardial oxygen supply. It decreases afterload, which decreases myocardial oxygen demand, therefore increasing cardiac output. The balloon inflates during diastole to increase blood flow to the coronary arteries. Keep the head of the bed < 30 degrees.

stem: A patient with which disorder may require a ventricular assist device (VAD)?
options: Prolonged QT interval leading to Torsades.
rationale: Indications for insertion of a ventricular assist device (VAD) are MI with cardiogenic shock, end stage cardiomyopathies, a "bridge" to cardiac transplant, and post cardiac bypass procedure.

stem: Which of the following parameters indicates successful resuscitation of the septic shock patient receiving fluids and norepinephrine?
rationale: A ScvO2 (RAP reading normal 70%) that is 0.70 or > indicates adequate oxygenation at the tissue level. An elevated lactate, SvO2 (mixed venous oxygen saturation with PA line -- normal 60-80%), and low CVP (normal 2-6 mm Hg) indicates inadequate tissue perfusion. Normal urine output in adults is 0.5-1.0 ml/hour.

stem: You are preparing to administer norepinephrine (Levophed) to a patient in septic shock. The nurse knows that the hemodynamic effects of this medication include which of the following?
options: Increasing blood pressure and systemic vascular resistance (SVR)
Decreasing heart rate and mean arterial pressure (MAP)
Increasing right (RAP) and left atrial pressures (PAP)
Decreasing stroke volume and pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR)
rationale: Norepinephrine increases systemic vascular resistance through vasoconstriction, which increases blood pressure. Potent vasoconstriction is needed in distributive shock.

stem: What is the preferred trigger for an intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP)?
rationale: The IABP automatically uses the ECG as a trigger. If it becomes unavailable it will use another trigger, such as the arterial waveform.

stem: Which intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) timing error is considered the worst and results in increased myocardial oxygen demand as the left ventricle pumps against increased pressures in the aorta?
rationale: Late deflation impedes ejection of the left ventricle as it attempts to pump against an inflated balloon.

stem: When the IABP is properly placed via femoral insertion, the tip of the catheter should be:
options: Just distal to the left subclavian artery
Proximal to the left subclavian artery
rationale: Proper placement of the intra-aortic balloon is in the descending thoracic aorta with the tip 2 cm distal to the origin of the left subclavian artery, with the base above the renal arteries.

stem: What assessment findings are important for ensuring that the IABP catheter is in the correct position?
options: Heart sounds and left radial pulse.
Left brachial pulse and urine output.
rationale: Left brachial pulse and urine output. If the balloon is too high in the aorta, the left subclavian artery would be occluded, affecting the left brachial and radial pulse. If the balloon is too distal in the aorta, the renal arteries will be blocked, decreasing urine output.

stem: Rust colored flakes in the tubing of the IABP indicates:
rationale: Blood entering the balloon when ruptured will appear to look like rust flakes or coffee grounds. Rupture may also be evidenced by blood in the sheath or loss gas alarm on machine. If this occurs, stop the pump immediately, clamp the catheter, place patient in left lateral position. The IABP needs to be removed as soon as possible to avoid clotting on the sheath.

stem: Fomepizole is administered for ethylene glycol ingestion. The anticipated response is a/an:
rationale: Increasing pH is the anticipated response to the action of Fomepizole (Antizol). Ethylene glycol ingestion (antifreeze and coolants) causes a high anion gap metabolic acidosis, so the pH decreases. The treatment is effective if the acidosis resolves.

stem: The following are common signs/symptoms of digoxin toxicity, EXCEPT:
rationale: Miosis is not a sign of digoxin toxicity, but visual changes such as yellow-green halos may be a symptom. Irregular pulse (tachycardia or bradycardia) may be seen, as well as mental status changes, and nausea/vomiting/diarrhea.

stem: The following are signs/symptoms of an anticholinergic overdose (diphenhydramine, atrovent, scopolamine, Thorazine, Librium, jimson weed), EXCEPT:
rationale: Lacrimation and salivation are signs of cholinergic exposure, not anticholinergic exposure. Anticholinergic signs: "Hot as a Hare, Red as a Beet, Dry as a Bone, Blind as a Bat, and Mad as a Hatter" -- hot, dry, delirious. Treat with IV sodium bicarbonate for systemic alkalinization.

stem: Which lab value(s) will be decreased in thyroid storm?
rationale: Thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) is decreased in hyperthyroidism, while T3 and T4 are elevated. Beta-blockers such as labetalol are the mainstay of therapy, along with manual cooling while avoiding aspirin.

stem: Your patient ate shellfish and is experiencing an allergic reaction. Anaphylactic shock is which type of shock?
rationale: Anaphylactic shock is one of the distributive shocks (maldistribution due to vasodilation). Treat with Epinephrine IM in the anterolateral thigh, repeat every 5 to 15 minutes. Administer crystalloids and histamine blockers.

stem: Your patient has a history of Addison's disease. Which vital sign change is seen in adrenal crisis?
rationale: Hypotension is seen in adrenal crisis due to hypovolemic shock. Patient is hyponatremic and hypoglycemic, with hyperkalemia and hypercalcemia. Treat with IV hydrocortisone and fluid resuscitation.

stem: A cardinal sign of hyperosmolar hyperglycemic syndrome (HHS or HHNK) is:
options: Metabolic acidosis and ketones in the urine.
Markedly elevated serum glucose and altered mental status.
rationale: HHS is characterized by markedly elevated serum glucose levels, altered mental status, severe dehydration (fluid deficit of 10 liters), tachycardia, and hypotension. Treat with fluid resuscitation and insulin until the glucose is 250-300 mg/dL. Monitor closely for seizures. DKA is characterized by metabolic acidosis, ketones in urine, and Kussmaul respirations.

stem: A common laboratory finding in a patient with diabetes insipidus (DI) is:
rationale: In diabetes insipidus, there is low anti-diuretic hormone (ADH), elevated serum osmolality and sodium, and decreased urine specific gravity and osmolality (< 200 mOsmol). Treat with isotonic solution fluid resuscitation to at least match urine output and desmopressin acetate or vasopressin (ADH).

stem: You are caring for a patient in diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Which components should you use to determine the anion gap?
rationale: Na+, K+, Cl-, and HCO3-. Anion gap is the gap between the positive ions (sodium and potassium) and negative ions (chloride and bicarbonate). Normal anion gap is 12 plus or minus 4 mEq/L. Anion gap is high in lactic acidosis, DKA, toxic alcohol ingestion, and renal failure.

stem: You are transporting a 75-year-old female patient diagnosed with pneumonia who is intubated and on the ventilator. The high-pressure alarm begins sounding on the ventilator. The patient most likely requires:
rationale: ET suctioning to clear the secretions. If you cannot troubleshoot the problem, disconnect the ventilator, and assist breathing with a bag-mask device. Use the DOPE mnemonic to diagnose the reason for the alarm -- Displaced, Obstructed, Pneumothorax, Equipment problems.

stem: Diabetes insipidus (DI) is characterized by the following, EXCEPT:
rationale: Diabetes Insipidus is from a lack of ADH causing dilute polyuria (6-24 L/day). The patient is severely hypovolemic leading to hypotension (not hypertension), with hypernatremia. Central diabetes insipidus (DI) is commonly caused by head trauma or tumor. Neurogenic DI is seen in meningitis.

stem: The nurse knows that the cardinal sign of syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) is:
rationale: SIADH occurs from excess anti-diuretic hormone (ADH), which leads to water intoxication. Too much water causes a dilutional hyponatremia (serum sodium < 125 mEq/L), decreased urinary output, and hypo-osmolality (serum osmolality < 280 mOsm/kg). The highest risk of SIADH is seizures due to hyponatremia. Some causes of SIADH are pneumonia and tuberculosis.

stem: You are called for an interfacility transport for a patient being admitted to an ICU for disseminated intravascular coagulopathy (DIC) after placental abruption. DIC is:
options: A hemorrhagic episode from a lack of clotting mechanisms.
Widespread inappropriate clotting that triggers bleeding.
Diagnosed by an increased level of clotting factors.
rationale: DIC is widespread inappropriate clotting that results in consumption of clotting factors, triggering bleeding. DIC is secondary to another problem, such as sepsis, obstetric emergencies, pancreatitis, and liver failure. Petechiae may be the first indication of DIC. D-dimer is elevated, but the definitive test for DIC is elevated fibrin split products (FSP's). Treatment is focused on eliminating the underlying cause, and transfusion. Blood components, such as FFP, cryoprecipitate, and platelets are used to replace lost factors.

stem: The following are true concerning intra-abdominal pressure, EXCEPT:
options: Intra-abdominal hypertension (IAH) is defined as an IAP \> 12-15 mm Hg.
Abdominal perfusion pressure (APP) is SBP- IAP.
Abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS) is a sustained IAP \> 20 mm Hg, associated with new organ dysfunction.
Bladder pressure most closely approximates intraperitoneal pressure.
rationale: Abdominal perfusion pressure (APP) is calculated as MAP -- IAP, not SBP -- IAP. The other answers are correct. Abdominal compartment syndrome early signs are increased peak airway pressures and decreased urine output. Management includes maintaining head transducer at symphysis pubis and treat if pressure is > 12-15mm Hg. Management includes maintaining head of bed up or in reverse trendelenburg position, loosening constructing dressings, preventing over-hydration, inserting a gastric tube to decompress the abdomen, and surgery.

stem: The priority when caring for a patient experiencing a seizure includes:
options: Protecting the patient from injury and administering benzodiazepines.
Intubating the apneic patient and administering Phenytoin.
Obtaining laboratory studies including a drug screen to determine the cause of the seizure.
Elevating the patient's head 30-45 degrees to decrease intracranial pressure.
rationale: Protecting the patient from physical injury and administering benzodiazepines. Most general tonic-clonic seizures are self-limiting, lasting < 3 minutes. Maintain safety and protect the airway by turning the patient on their side. Administer benzodiazepines and prevent further seizures with Phenytoin (Dilantin). Utilize RSO to intubate and continue sedation during transport.

stem: A patient had cytomegalovirus 2 weeks ago that resulted in a tingling sensation in the feet. She presented to the emergency department today with symmetrical ascending paralysis and is being transferred to a regional specialty center. The nurse knows these symptoms are suggestive of which disorder:
rationale: Symmetrical ascending paralysis and depressed or absent deep tendon reflexes are indicative of Guillain-Barre syndrome. Highest priority is to monitor for breathing effectiveness due to diaphragmatic involvement. Treat with IV immunoglobulins or plasmapheresis. Botulism causes descending paralysis.

stem: In the hypothermic patient, which of the following is a potential ECG finding?
rationale: "J" or Osborn waves (positive deflection at the beginning of the ST segment "J point") may be seen in severe hypothermia, as well as bradyarrhythmia's, prolonged PR, QRS, and QT, ventricular ectopy, and cardiac arrest.

stem: Which of the following are the most common symptoms of active pulmonary tuberculosis?
options: Hemoptysis, fever, night sweats, weight loss
rationale: Symptoms of active pulmonary tuberculosis include cough lasting 3 weeks or longer, hemoptysis, fever, weight loss, night sweats. Place patient on airborne precautions, with the crew wearing N95 masks and the patient wearing a surgical mask. Maximize fresh air exchange in a ground transport vehicle.

stem: You are transporting a patient who has carbon monoxide poisoning to a hyperbaric chamber for treatment. What treatment should be provided for this patient during transport?
options: Fluid boluses to keep urine output \> 50 mL/hour.
rationale: The treatment of acute carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning exposure is 100% oxygen delivery via a non-rebreather. The patient may be intubated if necessary. Providing high concentrations of oxygen causes carboxyhemoglobin to dissociate and convert to oxyhemoglobin. Pulse oximetry cannot differentiate between oxygen and carbon monoxide on hemoglobin. Pregnant patients are more likely to require hyperbaric oxygenation.

stem: A 30-year-old female who smokes and takes birth control complains of sudden onset dyspnea. Assessment reveals tachypnea, tachycardia, apprehension, and diaphoresis. The nurse expects the blood gases to reveal which combination?
rationale: Arterial blood gas will reveal a decreased PaO2 and a decreased PaCO2 in pulmonary embolus. A pulmonary embolus is a VQ mismatch where the patient complains of sudden onset dyspnea and pleuritic pain. The S2 heart sound may be accentuated.

stem: A patient is three days post-op pelvic fracture repair after an abrupt onset of pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, hemoptysis, and jugular vein distention. ECG shows onset right bundle branch block and right axis deviation. Which of the following is the "gold standard" in diagnosing a pulmonary embolus?
rationale: The only conclusive diagnostic test for pulmonary embolus is CT pulmonary angiography.

stem: A patient is transported to the emergency department with fatigue, abdominal pain, nausea, and jaundice. The nurse knows Hepatitis B (HBV) is transmitted by:
options: Contact with infected blood or body fluids.
rationale: Hepatitis B (HBV) is transmitted by contact with infected blood or body fluids, such as saliva, cerebrospinal fluid, amniotic fluid, semen, and vaginal secretions. Hepatitis A and E are transmitted via the fecal-oral route. Typical symptoms include nausea and vomiting, fatigue, malaise, RUQ abdominal pain, dark urine, gray stools, and jaundice. The most effective prevention is the hepatitis B vaccine.

stem: You are treating a patient with sepsis induced disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). Which lab value indicates the patient is improving?
rationale: DIC is a dysfunction of the clotting cascade. Diagnostics reveal elevated PT and PTT, low fibrinogen and platelet levels, and elevated D-dimer and fibrin split products. Treatment includes transfusions of FFP, platelets, cryoprecipitate, and packed RBC's. The patient is improving when platelet levels stabilize.

stem: A clinical symptom highly characteristic of human scabies is:
options: Red lesions that follow a dermatome.
Vesicular lesions that appear in crops.
rationale: The major symptom of scabies is nocturnal pruritis, itching that intensifies at night, along with threadlike burrows. Scabies is transmitted by direct contact, so wash all linen used in hot water and dry in hot dryer. The cabin should be vacuumed and cleaned thoroughly after patient transport.

stem: A group of migrant workers are decontaminated and treated with Atropine and Pralidoxime (2-PAM) after accidental exposure to insecticides. The treatment plan has been deemed effective when there is:
options: An increase in level of consciousness
rationale: Organophosphate pesticide (OP) toxicity causes a cholinergic excess, resulting in salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, emesis, miosis, increased bronchial secretions, and bradycardia (SLUDGE mnemonic). IV atropine is administered until there is clearing of bronchial secretions. Nicotinic effects of OP pesticides cause fasciculations and muscle weakness, treated with pralidoxime (2-PAM). Ensure the patient is decontaminated thoroughly prior to transport and elevate the head of bed since increased airway secretions. Treat seizures with benzodiazepines.

stem: What is the antidote for acetaminophen toxicity?
rationale: N-acetylcysteine (Acetadote) is the antidote for acetaminophen toxicity. Activated charcoal may be useful if administered within a few hours of ingestion only. Physostigmine may be administered in anticholinergic overdose. Glucagon may be used in beta-blocker and calcium-channel blocker toxicity. Methylene blue may be used in methemoglobinemia from benzocaine toxicity.

stem: The nurse has calculated the 0.9 mg/kg dose of tissue plasminogen activator (r-TPA) for the 60 kg patient as 54 mg total. What is the bolus dose to be administered over 1 minute?
rationale: 5.4 mg is 10% of the total r-TPA dose of 54 mg for a 60 kg patient. Tissue plasminogen activator dose is 0.9 mg/kg, bolus 10%, with the remainder over 1 hour.

stem: The nurse anticipates administering oral iodine for thyroid storm in which sequence?
options: Oral iodine should be the first medication administered.
Administer oral iodine immediately after beta-blocker.
Administer iodine at least one hour after the propylthiouracil (PTU) administration.
Aspirin should be the first medication, then beta-blocker, PTU, and iodine.
rationale: Administer the iodine at least one hour after the PTU, if given earlier it can increase T4 levels. Beta-blockers are administered first, then antithyroid medications such as propylthiouracil to block thyroid hormone synthesis. Aspirin is contraindicated in thyroid storm.

stem: A patient with a history of alcohol abuse complains of severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, fever, and vomiting. White blood cell count, amylase and lipase levels are elevated so the nurse suspects which disease process?
rationale: Epigastric pain radiating to the back, with elevated amylase and lipase levels are indicated of pancreatitis. Treat with fluid resuscitation and pain management.

stem: A patient who overdosed on Diltiazem (Cardizem) exhibits tetanic muscle contractions, numbness and tingling of fingers, and hyperactive deep tendon reflexes. The ECG shows a prolonged QT interval. The nurse anticipates administering:
rationale: Treatment of calcium-channel blocker overdose includes fluid resuscitation and vasopressors to treat bradycardia and hypotension, calcium gluconate or chloride, glucagon, insulin, and lipids.

stem: Your patient has esophageal varices from portal hypertension secondary to liver disease. Gastroesophageal variceal bleeding is treated with which of the following?
rationale: Gastroesophageal variceal bleeding is treated with IV vasopressin or Octreotide (Sandostatin). Monitor for tremors which may indicate alcohol withdrawal, leading to seizures.

stem: Which of the following treatment modalities is appropriate for the patient who overdosed on Elavil (amitriptyline)?
options: Induced vomiting to reduce the absorption of the medication.
Gastric lavage to eliminate the medication.
Sodium bicarbonate to promote systemic alkalinization.
rationale: Systemic alkalinization (urine alkalinization) with sodium bicarbonate can narrow the QRS, improve blood pressure, and control ventricular dysrhythmias seen in tricyclic antidepressants overdoses. Induced vomiting and gastric lavage are contraindicated because CNS depression can develop rapidly. Do not treat ventricular dysrhythmias with type 1A antidysrhythmic agents. Tricyclic antidepressants can cause a depressed mental status, cardiac conduction toxicity, and seizures. A terminal R wave may be seen in ECG lead aVR. OB Emergencies • Pregnancy-induced (Gestational) HTN -- high blood pressure, sudden weight gain, headache, proteinuria. Lower blood pressure and infuse magnesium sulfate to reduce seizure risk. Monitor for HELLP syndrome. • Eclampsia -- gestational HTN progressed to seizures. Treat with benzodiazepines, magnesium sulfate, and prepare for c-section. • Postpartum hemorrhage -- Treat with manual uterine (fundal) massage, oxytocin (Pitocin), tranexamic acid (TXA), and blood transfusions while treating the cause.

stem: The hallmark sign of pertussis is:
rationale: The hallmark sign of pertussis "whooping cough" is a paroxysmal cough. Inspiratory stridor is also heard in upper airway obstruction like croup. Expiratory wheeze is heard early in lower airway obstruction like asthma, or FBAO if unilateral. Drooling with respiratory distress and toxic appearance is the hallmark sign of epiglottitis. Pertussis is highly contagious, especially during the catarrhal stage, so keep the child on droplet isolation.

stem: Prone positioning may be the best transport posture for a child with which congenital anomaly?
rationale: Pierre Robin sequence (syndrome) is characterized by an underdeveloped jaw, retraction of the tongue, and tracheomalacia. Prone positioning may keep the tongue from obstructing the airway in less severe cases. Choanal atresia increases risk of respiratory distress. Aortic coarctation leads to increased blood pressures in the upper extremities than the lower extremities. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is a thickening of the ventricles, which more commonly causes sudden death in adolescents.

stem: Which is the best indicator of shock in pediatric patients?
rationale: Tachycardia is the best indication of shock in the pediatric patient. Tachypnea is seen in respiratory distress and shock. Capillary refill would be delayed, not decreased. Hypotension and bradycardia are late and ominous signs of shock in children. Fluid boluses for pediatric patients are isotonic fluids at 20 ml/kg.

stem: What is the most reliable indicator of fetal well-being?
rationale: "Variability" is the most reliable indicator of fetal well-being, based on electronic fetal monitor interpretation. Fetal movement is the most reliable non-monitor sign. Normal fetal heart rate is 110-160 beats/minute. Fundal height in centimeters approximates the gestation of the pregnancy in weeks.

stem: A woman just delivered her baby in the back of the ambulance and continues to have active vaginal bleeding. The initial nursing intervention is to:
options: Type and screen for blood administration.
rationale: The initial intervention is to vigorously massage the fundus, then administer IV oxytocin (Pitocin) 20-40 units in 1 liter of LR or methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM or IV to promote uterine atony. Administer morphine or fentanyl for pain.

stem: Why did the Pediatric Emergency Care Applied Research Network (PECARN) create guidelines for performing head CT scans?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is preferred in pediatric patients.
Ionizing radiation increases the risk of solid-organ cancers.
To follow the National Emergency X-radiography Utilization Study (NEXUS) criteria.
CT scans are expensive and delay transfer.
rationale: Ionizing radiation increases the risk of solid-organ cancers. PECARN criteria are set for under age 2 and 2 to 18 years of age. Indications for head CT include (but not limited to) CGS < 15, fall > 3 feet, palpable skull fracture, vomiting.

stem: You are on the scene where an infant was left in a car on a hot day. What is your priority action?
options: Initiate IV access to administer benzodiazepines to prevent seizure.
Intubate to administer cool, humidified oxygen.
Report to child protective services since all healthcare professionals are mandated reporters of child maltreatment.
Misting with water and manually fanning for evaporative cooling.
rationale: Priority treatment for hyperthermia is misting and fanning, while someone applies ice packs to the axilla and groin. Then initiate IV access and administer intravenous fluids at room temperature. Healthcare providers are mandated reporters of maltreatment, but that is not the priority.

stem: While you are assessing patients involved in an MVC, you encounter a 7-month pregnant patient complaining of sudden onset of painful uterine contractions, backache, and dark red vaginal bleeding. Her abdomen is rigid, HR is 140, and BP is 74/40. The nurse suspects:
rationale: Placental abruption is characterized by sudden onset of severe abdominal to back pain that is disproportionate to contractions, with loss of variability. There may be scant amounts of dark red blood or no visible bleeding. The abdomen may be "board-like" with a rising fundal height if severe hemorrhage. A severe complication of abruption is HELLP (hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelet count).

stem: A pregnant patient is being transferred to a high-risk OB unit for gestational hypertension. She is receiving a magnesium sulfate intravenous infusion. The following are priorities to monitor during magnesium administration, EXCEPT?
rationale: Magnesium does not alter pupil size. Monitor deep tendon reflexes, respiratory rate, and blood pressure closely during administration. Stop magnesium if respirations decrease < 12 and administer 1 gram of calcium gluconate 10% over at least 3 minutes. A normal magnesium level is 4.8-8.4 mg/dL. Magnesium toxicity is 10 mg/dL.

stem: The patient is in a breech presentation and delivery appears to be halted upon the delivery of the head. The appropriate action would be to
options: Initiate rapid transport, placing mother in a knee-chest position.
rationale: Mauriceau's maneuver is a method used in assisting a breech delivery in which the infant's body is supported by the right forearm while traction is made upon the shoulders by the left hand, maintaining the fetal head in a flexed position. The index and middle fingers are placed over the maxillary prominence on either side of the nose.

stem: Which of the following fetal heart monitoring strips indicates fetal hypoxia and constitutes an emergency? (Visual question -- strip C shows late decelerations)
options: Strip A -- reassuring pattern with FHR variability
Strip B -- early decelerations mirroring contractions
Strip C -- late decelerations occurring after peak of contraction
Strip D -- variable decelerations, V or W shaped
rationale: Late decelerations are a transient decrease in heart rate occurring at or after the peak of a uterine contraction, which indicates uteroplacental insufficiency and fetal hypoxia. A depicts a reassuring pattern which shows FHR variability. B depicts early decelerations in which the decelerations mirror the contraction and are caused by vagal stimulation from head compression. D depicts variable decelerations which can occur at any time during the contraction, are often V or W shaped, and are often caused by umbilical cord compression.

stem: When transporting an obese patient by rotor wing aircraft what intervention can be used to reduce the risk of barobariatrauma?
options: Maintain cabin pressure above 2,000 feet MSL.
Place a nasal cannula on patient during transport.
Administer high-flow oxygen for 15 minutes prior to lift-off.
Ensure that the patient has a c-collar in place prior to transport.
rationale: Barobariatrauma is a type of decompression sickness that occurs in obese patients during ascent to higher altitudes since fatty tissue contains more nitrogen. To prevent this, administer high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather for 15 minutes prior to lift-off and maintain oxygen saturation above 95% throughout transport.

stem: Gestational hypertension is characterized by which of the following?
rationale: Gestational HTN is characterized by new onset hypertension (140/90), proteinuria, and edema. Eclampsia is the development of tonic clonic seizures. Gestational hypertension may lead to HELLP syndrome, so monitor for RUQ abdominal pain (liver), bruising, and petechiae. Keep lights dim and administer magnesium sulfate to raise the seizure threshold. Treat HTN with labetalol or hydralazine.

stem: You are preparing to transport a patient at 28-weeks' gestation who is in preterm labor to a hospital with high-risk OB and NICU capabilities. The referring RN states that prior to transport she must administer betamethasone that was ordered. You know that the purpose of this medication is to:
options: Relax the uterus and decrease contractions.
rationale: Betamethasone is a glucocorticoid steroid given to stimulate fetal lung maturation and decrease the risk of ARDS in the preterm infant. It can also decrease mortality from intracranial hemorrhage in premature infants. It is administered as an IM injection to the pregnant mother. Dexamethasone is similar and can also be used.

stem: Nitrazine paper can be used to detect the presence of amniotic fluid to determine whether the membranes have ruptured. What color will the paper turn if amniotic fluid is present?
rationale: Nitrazine paper will turn dark blue if the pH is above 7.0, which may indicate that amniotic fluid is present. The vaginal pH and urine are acidic. The paper may also turn blue if bacterial vaginosis is present, which can elevate the vaginal pH.

stem: Which tocolytic medication would NOT be appropriate to administer to a patient in preterm labor who has a history of myasthenia gravis?
rationale: Magnesium sulfate interferes with the release of acetylcholine, so it is contraindicated in patients with myasthenia gravis. Use cautiously in patients with renal insufficiency and consult medical control if the urine output decreases to < 30 ml/hour.

stem: You arrive at the scene of an MVC and treat a 25-year-old patient who is 29 weeks pregnant. Upon assessing the patient, you palpate fetal parts over the abdomen. What do you suspect as the diagnosis?
rationale: A patient with signs of shock, shoulder pain, and palpable fetal parts are suggestive of uterine rupture. It is seen more often in rapid deceleration MVC's in patients who previously had a classic vertical cut cesarean-section.

stem: When preparing to intubate a pediatric patient with succinylcholine, which medication may be administered to prevent bradycardia?
rationale: Pediatric patients are more susceptible to bradycardia due to succinylcholine administration. Atropine may be used as a pretreatment to prevent bradycardia.

stem: Which drug may be administered to a 30-week premature infant in respiratory distress?
rationale: Respiratory distress (hyaline membrane disease) in preterm infants is often caused by a deficiency of surfactant. Surfactant decreases the surface tension in the alveoli during expiration, preventing atelectasis.

stem: You are preparing to intubate a 4-year-old patient. What size cuffed endotracheal (ET) tube do you anticipate using for intubation?
rationale: 4.5 Fr. The formula for estimating uncuffed endotracheal tube size in pediatric patients over 2 years of age is (16 + age in years) divided by 4. Select a cuffed tube that is one-half smaller than predicted uncuffed size. Cuffed tubes are advised to minimize tube movement, increase accuracy of waveform capnography, and to decrease air leak. ETT depth in cm is 3x the ETT size. Gastric tube can be estimated by multiplying the ETT size by 2.

stem: Which medication may be used to accelerate the closure of a patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) in a neonate?
rationale: Indomethacin, ibuprofen, and oxygen can be given to accelerate the closure of a PDA. Prostaglandin E1 can be used to maintain an open PDA. Common side effects of this medication include apnea and hypoventilation, so the patient must be monitored closely.

stem: What is the purpose of positioning the gravid patient in shock in either the left or right lateral recumbent position?
options: To decrease hemorrhage in the shock patient.
To prevent inferior vena cava syndrome.
rationale: To prevent inferior vena cava syndrome. The inferior vena cava is compressed when the gravid patient is supine, so tilt 15 to 30 degrees on either side to increase venous return, cardiac output, and blood pressure. If the patient is on a backboard, place towel rolls under the board to tilt the patient.

stem: All the following are anomalies present in patients with Tetralogy of Fallot, EXCEPT:
rationale: Patent ductus arteriosus. The four anomalies present in Tetralogy of Fallot are pulmonary stenosis, ventricular septal defect, right ventricular hypertrophy, and overriding aorta.

stem: How can the nurse assist in a precipitate delivery with shoulder dystocia?
options: Position the patient in the left lateral recumbent position.
Administer magnesium sulfate to slow the contractions.
Use McRoberts maneuver of positioning the patient in knee-chest position.
Position the patient in trendelenburg position.
rationale: The McRoberts maneuver increases the diameter of the pelvis until an episiotomy can be performed.

stem: Which x-ray finding would you expect to see in a patient presenting with croup (laryngotracheitis)?
rationale: "Steeple sign" or tracheal narrowing on a chest x-ray is suggestive of croup. Croup is seen most often in children 6 months to 3 years of age. Signs and symptoms include a history of an upper respiratory infection, low-grade fever, a barky cough, and inspiratory stridor.
